The Coding Agent Harness: Why Context Engineering Beats Model Shopping

Mar 4, 2026
Vibe Sparking

The Coding Agent Harness: Why Context Engineering Beats Model Shopping

Section titled “The Coding Agent Harness: Why Context Engineering Beats Model Shopping”

Your team adopted an AI coding agent last month. The first week felt electric. By week three, the pattern became clear: sometimes the agent nails it, sometimes it produces code that looks plausible but crumbles under review. Duplicate logic in three places. A helper function recreated from scratch when one already exists in utils. An N+1 query hiding inside a loop.

You start wondering if you picked the wrong model.

You didn’t. The model was never the problem.

The Insight Most Teams Miss

Section titled “The Insight Most Teams Miss”

Julian de Angelis at MercadoLibre has been deploying AI coding agents across nearly 20,000 developers and thousands of repositories. His conclusion after operating at that scale:

Your AI coding agent is a brilliant new hire on day one. Fast, eager, and with zero context about your codebase, your conventions, or your architecture.

You wouldn’t hand a new engineer a Jira ticket that says “fix this bug” and walk away. You’d onboard them: docs, tech stack overview, style guides, release workflows, access to internal tools, a mentor to review their work.

That onboarding system is what de Angelis calls the agent harness - the structured set of controls that shape what the agent knows, what it can do, and how its work gets validated. The underlying discipline is context engineering: designing and controlling everything an LLM sees before it generates a single token.

The Constraint Nobody Talks About

Section titled “The Constraint Nobody Talks About”

Every popular AI coding agent - Claude Code, Cursor, Codex - runs the same fundamental loop:

Read -> Plan -> Code -> Validate -> Iterate

The agent reads your files, plans an approach, writes code, runs tests, and loops back if something fails. At every iteration, the context window grows. System prompts, tool definitions, conversation history, file contents, terminal output, the agent’s own generated code - all of it accumulates in a finite workspace.

Here’s the part that surprises people: past roughly 60% utilization of the context window, more context makes the agent actively worse. This phenomenon is called context rot. Even with windows reaching 1M tokens, the fundamental challenge remains. Every token spent on a verbose tool result or an overly long rules file is a token not available for the actual task.

The best agent harnesses are designed with this reality at the center: inject the right context at the right moment, and avoid polluting the window with anything else.

Four Levers That Actually Matter

Section titled “Four Levers That Actually Matter”

1. Custom Rules

Section titled “1. Custom Rules”

Files like CLAUDE.md, cursor/rules, or AGENTS.md get injected into the agent’s context at the start of every interaction. This is the most accessible lever.

What goes in:

  • Tech stack and architecture patterns
  • Naming conventions and code style
  • Testing philosophy (“always write unit tests with table-driven inputs”)
  • Common pitfalls specific to your codebase
  • Anti-patterns you’ve seen the agent produce repeatedly

What doesn’t belong:

  • Entire API documentation (too long, wastes context)
  • Obvious instructions (“write clean code”)
  • Contradictory rules

Practical tips:

  • Keep them under 500 lines. Precision matters.
  • Split rules into focused files by concern: architecture, testing, code style, security. The agent loads only what’s relevant.
  • Include few-shot examples. Models learn from examples better than abstract instructions.
  • Make them living documents. Every time the agent makes a mistake, update the rules. Over weeks, this file becomes remarkably effective.

2. MCP Servers

Section titled “2. MCP Servers”

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are plugins that give the agent tools beyond reading and writing files. Without them, the agent is limited to what’s in the repo. With them, it can:

  • Query your database to understand schema and data
  • Search internal documentation or wikis
  • Look up API contracts
  • Interact with CI/CD pipelines
  • Access design specs from Figma
  • Validate its own output against real systems

MCP servers bridge the gap between the agent and your organization’s knowledge. They give the agent access to the same resources a human developer would use.

3. Skills

Section titled “3. Skills”

Skills give the agent new capabilities and domain expertise. A skill is a directory with an entrypoint file that can bundle templates, example outputs, reference docs, and executable scripts.

The design is clever: only a short description stays in context permanently, so the agent knows what’s available. The full content loads only when the skill is invoked - by the user or automatically when the agent detects relevance. This on-demand loading means skills can be as detailed as needed without permanently consuming context budget.

They come in two flavors:

  • Reference skills inject knowledge: conventions, patterns, domain context
  • Task skills provide step-by-step instructions for specific actions

The real power is that skills can bundle and execute scripts, run in isolated subagents with their own clean context window, and compose together. Programmable, context-aware, composable units of agent behavior.

4. Spec-Driven Development

Section titled “4. Spec-Driven Development”

This one reframes how you interact with agents entirely.

Consider this prompt: “Make a new feature to add new items from the backoffice.”

Seems simple. But it says nothing about: which tech stack, where the backoffice is, API contracts, storage layer, idempotency handling, authorization rules.

The agent will read the codebase, plan something reasonable, and produce a working MVP. You add an item and it works. You click “add” again and it inserts the item twice. It was “obvious” the agent should have managed idempotency by ID. In your business, it’s “obvious” that only one role can insert items.

The problem isn’t the agent. It’s the bare initial input. The bottleneck sits between the chair and the screen.

Spec-Driven Development (SDD) solves this by writing detailed specifications before the agent writes code. What the feature does, how it integrates, edge cases, acceptance criteria, test plans. The spec becomes the harness - it consolidates architecture decisions, guidance, and review criteria into a single artifact that engineers the agent’s entire context window in one shot.

And you can use the agent to write the specs too.

The Feedback Loop

Section titled “The Feedback Loop”

The four levers define what the agent knows and can do. The piece that makes the whole system converge is the feedback loop: tests, linters, type checkers, build scripts. Every tool that produces a pass/fail signal becomes a self-correction mechanism.

Agent hooks make this enforceable. They are user-defined commands that execute automatically at specific points in the agent’s lifecycle. Wire your validation into a Stop hook, and the agent literally cannot finish until the checks pass. Not a suggestion it might ignore. An enforced gate.

The more structured feedback available, the less human intervention is needed.

MercadoLibre’s Playbook at 20,000 Developers

Section titled “MercadoLibre’s Playbook at 20,000 Developers”

Theory is fine. Here’s what works at scale with 9+ technology stacks, thousands of legacy repositories, and multiple knowledge domains:

Standardized rules per technology. A curated set of custom rules at the org level, tuned to each tech stack, internal libraries, and code review standards. Teams inherit the base and layer on repo-specific rules. Getting the specificity right is critical - too specific breaks legacy projects, too loose lets unapproved libraries through (security risk).

Internal MCP servers. Built-in access to internal cloud platform capabilities, internal SDKs, business context via RAG, and updated application documentation.

Custom code review agents. Standalone review agents running in CI that analyze every PR - human-written or AI-generated - before a human reviewer sees it. They return prioritized findings so engineers see critical issues first. Human reviewers focus on design decisions instead of catching style violations. Faster review cycles, more consistent quality, and a safety net that scales with PR volume, not reviewer headcount.

Start Here

Section titled “Start Here”

If you haven’t built an agent harness yet, start with the simplest lever: custom rules. Create a CLAUDE.md or equivalent file with your coding conventions, common pitfalls, and a couple of concrete examples of good and bad patterns in your codebase.

Run it for a week. Every time the agent produces something wrong, add a rule. By the end of the month, that file will have quietly transformed your results more than any model upgrade could.

The teams that win with AI coding agents won’t be the ones using the fanciest model. They’ll be the ones with the best-engineered harness.

Based on Julian de Angelis’s analysis of AI coding agent deployment at MercadoLibre. Original thread.

Tags:
  • Claude Code
  • Context Engineering
  • AI Coding
  • MCP
  • Spec Driven Development
  • Developer Productivity
  • Agent Harness

Claude Agent SDK: From Weekend Hacks to Production-Ready Agents

Feb 2, 2026
Vibe Sparking

The Weekend That Changed Everything

Section titled “The Weekend That Changed Everything”

Nick Spisak spent a weekend pushing Claude Agent SDK to its limits. What started as casual experimentation turned into a deep dive through the SDK’s internals—and he came back with six patterns that separate toy demos from production-grade agents.

Here’s what he found.

1. Lifecycle Hooks: Your Agent’s Guardrails

Section titled “1. Lifecycle Hooks: Your Agent’s Guardrails”

The SDK exposes three hooks that let you intercept agent behavior:

  • PreToolUse — runs before any tool call. Perfect for validation, rate limiting, or blocking dangerous operations.
  • PostToolUse — runs after tool results return. Audit logs, result transformation, cost tracking.
  • Stop — graceful shutdown. Clean up resources, save state, close connections.

Without these hooks, you’re flying blind. With them, you control what your agent can actually do.

2. Skill Hot-Reload: Update Without Restart

Section titled “2. Skill Hot-Reload: Update Without Restart”

Agents accumulate context over long sessions. Killing them to deploy a fix means losing all that context.

The /reload-skills command solves this. One command reloads skill definitions from disk while keeping the session alive. No restart, no context loss.

I’ve used this to iterate on a skill five times in one debugging session. The agent kept its memory of what we’d tried before.

3. Session Teleportation: Move Your Agent

Section titled “3. Session Teleportation: Move Your Agent”

Sometimes you need to move a running session to another machine. Maybe you’re switching laptops. Maybe the session started on a server and you want it local.

/teleport session-id serializes the full session state—conversation history, tool states, everything—and reconstructs it elsewhere. The agent picks up exactly where it left off.

This works across machines. I’ve teleported sessions from my MacBook to a Linux server mid-conversation.

4. Dynamic MCP Loading: Solve Context Pollution

Section titled “4. Dynamic MCP Loading: Solve Context Pollution”

Here’s the problem: loading all 50 MCP servers at startup dumps thousands of tool descriptions into your context window. Most calls only use 2-3 tools. You’re paying tokens for tools you never touch.

The fix: ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=true

Instead of loading everything upfront, the agent searches for relevant tools on demand. Nick measured an 85% reduction in token overhead with this approach.

The tradeoff? Slightly higher latency on first tool use while it searches. Worth it for most workloads.

5. Custom Agents: Automatic Delegation

Section titled “5. Custom Agents: Automatic Delegation”

Create specialized agents in .claude/agents/ and the main agent learns when to delegate.

Each file defines expertise, available tools, and handoff triggers. The main agent routes tasks automatically. A code review request goes to the reviewer. A “check for vulnerabilities” goes to the auditor.

You get specialization without manual routing.

6. Security Architecture: Prompt Injection Resistance

Section titled “6. Security Architecture: Prompt Injection Resistance”

Claude 4.5 includes improved defenses against prompt injection attacks. Testing shows the success rate dropped from 23.6% to 11.2%.

That’s not zero. Don’t blindly trust user input. But it means the model catches more injection attempts before they execute.

Layer your defenses: PreToolUse hooks for high-risk operations, output filtering for sensitive data, and rate limiting for external API calls.

Boris Cherny’s Power-User Patterns

Section titled “Boris Cherny’s Power-User Patterns”

Boris Cherny (author of Programming TypeScript) shared his workflow for getting more out of Claude:

Run 3-5 git worktrees in parallel. Each worktree gets its own Claude session. You can work on multiple features simultaneously without context switching. The sessions stay isolated.

Start complex tasks with plan mode. Don’t let the agent start coding immediately. Ask for a plan first. Review it. Then execute. You catch bad approaches before wasting time.

Update CLAUDE.md after every correction. When you fix a mistake, add a note to CLAUDE.md explaining what went wrong and the correct approach. Future sessions learn from past sessions.

Turn repeated tasks into skills. If you’ve explained something three times, write it as a skill. The skill file becomes reusable across projects.

Use subagents to keep main context clean. Long-running tasks accumulate context. Spawn subagents for focused work, let them finish, then summarize results back to main. Your primary session stays lean.

What This Means in Practice

Section titled “What This Means in Practice”

These aren’t theoretical features. They’re solutions to real problems:

  • Lifecycle hooks → Your agent won’t accidentally rm -rf /
  • Hot-reload → You can fix bugs without losing a 2-hour context window
  • Teleportation → Your agent follows you across machines
  • Dynamic MCP → You stop burning tokens on unused tools
  • Custom agents → Specialists handle specialized work
  • Better security → Fewer injection attacks succeed

The SDK gives you the primitives. How you combine them depends on what you’re building.

Further Reading

Section titled “Further Reading”
  • Claude Agent SDK Documentation
  • Nick Spisak’s original thread: @NickSpisak_
  • Boris Cherny’s tips: @bcherny

What patterns are you finding useful? Hit me up—always looking for new tricks.

Tags:
  • AI
  • Claude
  • Agent SDK
  • Anthropic
  • MCP

Tessl: Giving Coding Agents the Context They Need to Succeed

Jan 25, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

If you’ve used coding agents in real projects, you know the pattern. They get 80% of the way there, then stumble on API specifics—using deprecated methods, inventing parameters that don’t exist, or making assumptions that break in production. The code compiles, but it’s wrong.

Tessl is addressing this problem head-on with a deceptively simple idea: give agents structured, versioned context about the libraries they’re using. The results are compelling—up to 3.3x improvement in correct API usage across 300+ open-source packages.

The Problem: API Misuse

Section titled “The Problem: API Misuse”

Agents fail in predictable ways. When an LLM encounters a library it doesn’t know well (or knows an outdated version of), it doesn’t admit ignorance—it guesses. This creates brittle code that:

  • Uses non-existent or deprecated APIs
  • Ignores established patterns and idioms
  • Requires multiple rounds of fixes
  • Breaks when dependencies update

Tessl calls this “abstraction adherence”—whether an agent uses a library’s intended public interfaces instead of improvising. It’s a metric that matters far more than whether code simply compiles.

How Tessl Works

Section titled “How Tessl Works”

Tessl provides “tiles”—structured, versioned documentation specifically designed for AI agents to consume. Each tile covers a specific library and includes:

  • Correct API usage patterns
  • Version-specific information
  • Idiomatic examples
  • Common pitfalls

The key innovation is that tiles integrate directly into your project as dependencies. When you run tessl sync in your project, Tessl automatically:

  1. Scans your package.json or requirements.txt
  2. Matches dependencies to available tiles
  3. Ensures tiles match your installed versions
  4. Makes this context available via MCP to any agent

The Results

Section titled “The Results”

Across 300+ open-source packages on npm and PyPI, Tessl tiles demonstrated:

  • Up to 3.3x improvement in abstraction adherence for poorly-known packages
  • 1.2x improvement overall
  • 35% fewer integration errors
  • 1.4x faster completion on API-heavy tasks

The biggest gains come from libraries that agents typically struggle with—niche packages, newer versions, or specialized use cases not well-represented in training data.

Registry and Evals

Section titled “Registry and Evals”

Tessl maintains a public registry of tiles covering thousands of open-source libraries. What’s unique is that each tile includes published evals—you can see exactly how well agents perform with that specific library.

This transparency serves two purposes:

  1. Teams can track impact over time as models improve
  2. Library authors can contribute authoritative tiles for their projects

The registry is free to explore and integrates with major agent platforms via MCP.

Spec-Driven Development

Section titled “Spec-Driven Development”

Beyond library tiles, Tessl enables teams to encode their own conventions:

  • Directory layout rules
  • Naming conventions
  • Code style preferences
  • Architecture patterns

This means every AI contribution follows team standards without manual enforcement.

Industry Perspective

Section titled “Industry Perspective”

The platform has garnered attention from industry leaders:

“Spec-Driven Development is the next frontier in AI coding.” — Olivier Pomel, CEO, DataDog

“Tessl is the perfect antidote to vibe coding.” — Martin Wimpress, Sr Software Engineer, Chainguard

“There’s a big gap between demo-ware and production-ware.” — Des Traynor, Co-Founder, Intercom

The consensus is that structure and discipline—exactly what Tessl provides—are missing ingredients for reliable AI-assisted development.

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”

Tessl offers a free tier to get started:

  1. Install the CLI: npm install -g @tessl/cli
  2. Run in your project: tessl sync
  3. Explore the registry at tessl.io/registry

The platform works with any agent supporting MCP, making it a flexible addition to existing AI workflows.

Looking Ahead

Section titled “Looking Ahead”

As AI agents become more embedded in development workflows (Gartner predicts 40% of enterprise apps will have task-specific agents by 2026), tools that provide reliable context will be critical.

Tessl’s approach—structured, versioned, eval-backed context—offers a path toward agents that don’t just write code, but write correct, maintainable code that follows team standards.

Sources:

  • Tessl Official Site
  • Fixing API Misuse: How Tessl Improves Agent Accuracy by up to 3.3X
  • Tessl Registry
  • Devops.com: Tessl Details Plan to Build Platform for Managing AI Agents
  • GitHub: tesslio
Tags:
  • AI-Assisted-Development
  • Tessl
  • Agent-Enablement
  • Spec-Driven-Development
  • MCP

Getting Started with Claude Agent SDK: Build Your Own AI Agents with Code

Jan 21, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Getting Started with Claude Agent SDK: Build Your Own AI Agents with Code

Section titled “Getting Started with Claude Agent SDK: Build Your Own AI Agents with Code”

Tired of duct-taping scripts, tools, and prompts together? Claude Agent SDK lets you transform Claude Code’s “plan-build-run” workflow into truly programmable agents for task automation, tool orchestration, and CLI app development - without writing mountains of glue code.

Overview and Key Features

Section titled “Overview and Key Features”

Claude Agent SDK is Anthropic’s official Python SDK that leverages Claude Code’s powerful capabilities, providing developers with a structured way to build AI agent applications.

Core Features

Section titled “Core Features”
  • Seamless Claude Code Integration: Uses the same engine as Claude Code for a consistent experience
  • MCP Protocol Support: Native support for Model Context Protocol, connecting to hundreds of external tools
  • Dual-Mode Interaction: Supports both simple query mode and bidirectional conversation mode
  • Async Streaming Interface: Built-in async support with real-time streaming responses
  • Tool Call Orchestration: Easily register and manage custom tools for complex workflows
  • Local Environment Integration: Direct access to local filesystem and development tools

Why Choose Claude Agent SDK?

Section titled “Why Choose Claude Agent SDK?”

Compared to other AI agent frameworks, Claude Agent SDK offers unique advantages:

FeatureClaude Agent SDKLangChainAutoGen
Official SupportAnthropic OfficialThird-partyMicrosoft Research
MCP SupportNativeVia adaptersNot supported
Learning CurveGentleModerateSteep
Claude OptimizationDeepGenericGeneric
Glue CodeMinimalModerateHeavy

Prerequisites, Installation & Configuration

Section titled “Prerequisites, Installation & Configuration”

System Requirements

Section titled “System Requirements”
  • Python: 3.10 or higher
  • Node.js: 18+ (for Claude Code CLI)
  • Claude API Key or a valid Anthropic account

Installing Claude Code CLI

Section titled “Installing Claude Code CLI”

Windows (PowerShell):

Then add the following path to your system environment variables:

macOS/Linux:

After installation, restart your terminal and verify:

On first run, use the claude command to authenticate.

Installing Claude Agent SDK

Section titled “Installing Claude Agent SDK”

Install the Python SDK via pip:

If you encounter a CLINotFoundError, make sure the Claude CLI is properly installed and added to your PATH.

Recommended Use Cases and Best Practices

Section titled “Recommended Use Cases and Best Practices”

Use Case 1: Automated Development Workflows

Section titled “Use Case 1: Automated Development Workflows”

Turn repetitive development tasks into automated agents:

Everyday Applications:

  • Automated code review and refactoring suggestions
  • Unit test coverage generation
  • Automated documentation generation and updates

Use Case 2: Trend Research Agent

Section titled “Use Case 2: Trend Research Agent”

Create a research assistant that automatically collects and analyzes market trends:

Use Case 3: Multi-Tool CLI Application

Section titled “Use Case 3: Multi-Tool CLI Application”

Build a complete command-line application:

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”
  1. Clear system prompts: Define the agent’s role and behavioral boundaries precisely
  2. Limit tool scope: Grant only the minimum tools required for the task
  3. Use async streaming: Improve response speed and user experience
  4. Error handling: Handle API errors and timeouts gracefully
  5. Cost monitoring: Track token usage to control costs

MCP Tool Integration

Section titled “MCP Tool Integration”

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a core feature of Claude Agent SDK that lets agents connect to external tools and data sources in a standardized way.

Built-in MCP Tools

Section titled “Built-in MCP Tools”

Custom MCP Tools

Section titled “Custom MCP Tools”

Create your own tools and register them with the SDK:

Popular MCP Integrations

Section titled “Popular MCP Integrations”
  • Database Connections: PostgreSQL, MongoDB, ClickHouse
  • Cloud Services: AWS, Google Cloud, Azure
  • API Integrations: GitHub, Slack, Notion
  • Development Tools: Git, Docker, Kubernetes

Important Notes, FAQ & Troubleshooting

Section titled “Important Notes, FAQ & Troubleshooting”

Important Notes

Section titled “Important Notes”
  1. API Quota Management: Monitor your API usage to avoid exceeding limits
  2. Sensitive Information Protection: Never include API keys or passwords in prompts
  3. Async Operations: Ensure proper use of async/await syntax
  4. Timeout Settings: Set reasonable timeouts for long-running tasks

FAQ

Section titled “FAQ”

Q: How do I get a Claude API Key?

A: Visit the Anthropic Console, create an account, and generate an API Key.

Q: Does the SDK support other programming languages?

A: In addition to Python, there is an official TypeScript/JavaScript version.

Q: How do I handle concurrent requests?

A: Use asyncio to create multiple client instances, each handling independent requests.

Q: Can it be used offline?

A: No, the SDK requires a connection to Anthropic’s API servers.

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

Issue 1: CLINotFoundError

Issue 2: Authentication Failure

Issue 3: Response Timeout

Comparison with Similar Products

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Products”

vs LangChain

Section titled “vs LangChain”

LangChain is a powerful AI application development framework supporting multiple model providers.

Choose LangChain if you:

  • Need to support multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Cohere, etc.)
  • Are building complex chain-based and RAG applications
  • Need a rich library of prebuilt components and integrations

Choose Claude Agent SDK if you:

  • Are focused on Claude models
  • Need seamless Claude Code integration
  • Want less configuration and glue code

vs AutoGen

Section titled “vs AutoGen”

AutoGen is a multi-agent conversation framework developed by Microsoft Research.

Choose AutoGen if you:

  • Are building complex multi-agent collaboration systems
  • Need automatic negotiation between agents
  • Are doing academic research or experimental projects

Choose Claude Agent SDK if you:

  • Need production-grade stability
  • Are building practical single-agent applications
  • Value code simplicity and maintainability

MCP Ecosystem Integration

Section titled “MCP Ecosystem Integration”

Claude Agent SDK is the official implementation of the MCP protocol, integrating seamlessly with the entire MCP ecosystem:

  • MCP Official Documentation
  • MCP Servers Repository
  • LangChain MCP Integration

References

Section titled “References”

Official Resources

Section titled “Official Resources”
  • Claude Agent SDK (Python) - GitHub
  • Claude Agent SDK Official Docs
  • MCP Protocol Documentation
  • Anthropic Official Website

Learning Resources

Section titled “Learning Resources”
  • Claude Agent SDK (Python) Learning Guide
  • Claude Agent SDK Practical Guide: Building Production-Ready AI Agents
  • Claude Agent SDK for Python - Jimmy Song
  • Claude Agent SDK Complete Development Guide

Community Resources

Section titled “Community Resources”
  • AWS: Claude Agent SDK Production Deployment
  • ClickHouse + Claude Agent SDK Integration
  • MCP Ultimate Guide

Framework Comparisons

Section titled “Framework Comparisons”
  • Top Agentic AI Frameworks in 2025
  • Choosing the Right AI Agent Framework in 2025
  • How to build AI agents with MCP: 12 framework comparison

Start your Claude Agent SDK journey! Begin with simple automation scripts and gradually build complex AI agent applications. If you’re already using Claude Code, the Agent SDK will be a powerful tool to boost your development productivity.

  • AI
  • Claude
  • Agent SDK
  • Python
  • MCP
  • Agent Development

Codex Skills: AI Agent Skill Library for Smarter Code Assistants

Jan 20, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Codex Skills: AI Agent Skill Library for Smarter Code Assistants

Section titled “Codex Skills: AI Agent Skill Library for Smarter Code Assistants”

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

am-will/codex-skills is a curated collection of open-source AI Agent skills designed to extend the capabilities of AI code assistants like OpenAI Codex and Claude Code.

Key Highlights

Section titled “Key Highlights”
  1. Unified Skill Standard: Follows the Agent Skills Open Standard - write once, use everywhere
  2. Rich Pre-built Skills: Covers browser automation, document querying, frontend best practices, and more
  3. Easy Installation: Supports one-click installation via npx globally or to specific projects
  4. Modular Design: Each skill is independently maintained and can be installed as needed

Links

Section titled “Links”
  • GitHub: am-will/codex-skills
  • Chinese Version: 中文版本
Tags:
  • AI Development
  • Codex
  • Claude Code
  • Agent Skills
  • MCP

Claude Code + iMessage: The Complete Cure for Message Procrastination

Jan 19, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

I have, historically, been embarrassingly bad at answering messages.

I either respond in 1 minute or 1 month. No in between.

A friend sends a text. I read it. I think “I should respond to this thoughtfully.” I put my phone down. Three weeks pass. Now it’s weird to respond. So I don’t. The relationship quietly erodes.

A client sends an email with a question. I know exactly what I need to say, but crafting the professional response feels like work. I’ll do it after I grab my coffee. Suddenly it’s the end of the day and my client has gone all day without a response.

This pattern has cost me more than I’d like to admit. Friendships that faded. Opportunities that disappeared. The guilt of unanswered messages piling up. The anxiety of opening my inbox.

I fixed this with Claude Code.

Yes, I know how that sounds. But hear me out.

Why This Isn’t Pathetic

Section titled “Why This Isn’t Pathetic”

Using AI to help respond to text messages sounds deeply unhinged. Like I’m some kind of antisocial robot who can’t handle basic human interaction.

That’s not what this is.

The problem isn’t a personality defect. It’s a quirk of how brains work. Once you understand it, using tools to work around it stops feeling pathetic and starts feeling like good strategy.

Why We Procrastinate on Replies

Section titled “Why We Procrastinate on Replies”
Psychological MechanismExplanation
Decision fatigueEvery message requires micro-decisions: What’s the right tone? How formal should I be? Should I apologize for the delay? Each decision depletes the same mental resource
Blank page problemWhen you can say anything, deciding what to say becomes overwhelming. Infinite possibilities create paralysis
Loss aversionWe’re wired to fear losses more than we value gains. Saying the wrong thing feels riskier than saying nothing. So we default to nothing
Working memory limitationsComposing a response requires holding multiple things in mind simultaneously: relationship context, message content, intended meaning, interpretation, word choice, tone. Human working memory caps around 4 items
Zeigarnik effectUnfinished tasks occupy mental space. Every unanswered message becomes a small cognitive burden

The Core Insight

Section titled “The Core Insight”

Reacting to a draft is dramatically easier than creating from scratch.

When someone shows you a paragraph and asks “does this sound right?”, you can immediately spot what’s off. But staring at a blank page trying to write that same paragraph? Torture.

Editing uses recognition. Creating uses recall. Recognition is fast and easy. Recall is slow and exhausting.

Claude gives you a draft to react to. You go from “what do I even say?” to “let me tweak this.”

Setting Up Claude Code on Mac

Section titled “Setting Up Claude Code on Mac”

On Mac, Claude Code can read your iMessages directly. Messages are stored locally in a SQLite database on your computer—Claude can query it without screenshots or copy-pasting.

Basic Usage

Section titled “Basic Usage”

Claude pulls the conversation. Then:

No screenshots. No switching apps. No friction.

What if You’re Not on Mac?

Section titled “What if You’re Not on Mac?”

Paste the message text directly, or drag in a screenshot—Claude can see images too.

The Context Advantage: MCPs

Section titled “The Context Advantage: MCPs”

Here’s where Claude Code pulls ahead of any other AI writing tool.

Claude Code can connect to external data sources through MCP (Model Context Protocol). This means when drafting a message, Claude isn’t working in a vacuum—it can pull in context from your actual life.

Practical Scenarios

Section titled “Practical Scenarios”
ScenarioMCP ConnectionResult
Someone asks “Are you free for lunch next week?”Calendar MCPClaude checks your real availability and drafts with specific times
Client emails about a projectNotes/Email MCPClaude pulls context from notes, previous emails, or project management tools
Friend mentions something you talked about months agoSearch historyClaude searches conversation history and picks up the thread naturally

Common MCP Connections

Section titled “Common MCP Connections”
  • Calendar — Know your real availability when scheduling
  • Email — Understand ongoing threads and context
  • Notes apps — Reference your own thinking and project details
  • CRM — Pull client history for professional responses
  • Slack — Context from team conversations

The difference is profound. Without MCPs, you’re asking Claude to draft a response to a message in isolation. With MCPs, Claude understands the full context—your schedule, your history with this person, your current projects, your notes—and drafts accordingly.

You stop providing context manually. Claude just knows.

Core Prompt Templates

Section titled “Core Prompt Templates”

iMessage (Mac) Prompt

Section titled “iMessage (Mac) Prompt”

For Other Platforms

Section titled “For Other Platforms”

Seven Real-World Patterns

Section titled “Seven Real-World Patterns”

Pattern 1: The Message You’ve Been Avoiding

Section titled “Pattern 1: The Message You’ve Been Avoiding”

You know the one. A friend reached out weeks ago. You meant to respond. Now it’s awkward.

Your prompt:

Follow-up:

Claude’s draft:

“I’m the worst—just seeing this. Yes, let’s actually do it though. How’s next Thursday or Friday after work? I’ll come to your neighborhood.”

What I actually sent:

“I’m the worst—just seeing this. Yes, let’s do it. Thursday or Friday work? I’ll come to you.”

90% of the message came from Claude. I tweaked two phrases. Total time: 30 seconds instead of three weeks of avoidance.

Pattern 2: The Client Email Requiring Diplomacy

Section titled “Pattern 2: The Client Email Requiring Diplomacy”

Professional communication is exhausting because every word carries weight.

The situation: A client is frustrated about a delayed project.

Your prompt:

You’ve told Claude exactly what the response needs to accomplish. It’s not guessing—it’s executing against clear criteria.

The mental load of “how do I handle this?” becomes “does this draft handle it?”

Pattern 3: The Networking Follow-Up

Section titled “Pattern 3: The Networking Follow-Up”

You met someone interesting. You should follow up. But what do you even say?

Your prompt:

Networking follow-ups die in drafts because we overthink them. Claude’s draft gives you something to react to—you can immediately see if the tone is right, if the ask is appropriate.

Pattern 4: The Difficult Workplace Message

Section titled “Pattern 4: The Difficult Workplace Message”

Pushing back on your boss. Giving critical feedback. Declining a request from a colleague who guilts you. These messages get postponed precisely because they’re hard.

The situation: Your boss wants you to take on another project. You’re overloaded.

Your prompt:

Claude gives you the hard thing said in a way that isn’t confrontational. You read it, feel relieved, adjust for your specific dynamics, and send.

Pattern 5: The Emotionally Sensitive Message

Section titled “Pattern 5: The Emotionally Sensitive Message”

Some messages require care. Condolences. Support during hard times. Congratulations that don’t sound hollow.

The situation: A colleague’s parent just passed away.

Your prompt:

These messages are hard because we’re afraid of saying the wrong thing. So we say nothing. Or we send something generic that doesn’t land.

Claude helps you find words when you can’t.

Pattern 6: The Scheduling Back-and-Forth

Section titled “Pattern 6: The Scheduling Back-and-Forth”

Not emotionally complex—just tedious. Coordinating plans. Rescheduling meetings. The endless logistical dance.

Without calendar MCP:

With calendar MCP:

Claude checks your real availability and drafts accordingly. No more manually cross-referencing your calendar, no more accidentally double-booking.

This is the lowest-stakes use case, but it adds up the most. These small messages take 30 seconds to write, but the friction of context-switching means they sit for hours.

Pattern 7: The Message You’re Dreading

Section titled “Pattern 7: The Message You’re Dreading”

Sometimes you need to say something genuinely difficult. Declining a wedding invitation. Breaking bad news. Ending a professional relationship. Setting a boundary with family.

The situation: You can’t afford to attend a friend’s destination wedding.

Your prompt:

The message that would have taken weeks to compose takes ten minutes. The relationship survives because you responded instead of avoiding.

Batch Processing Your Backlog

Section titled “Batch Processing Your Backlog”

Here’s where this becomes transformative.

Your prompt:

Claude surfaces your entire backlog in seconds. Then work through them:

In 20 minutes, you clear weeks of communication debt.

I do this every Sunday now. The psychological relief is enormous.

The Proactive System: Beyond Responding

Section titled “The Proactive System: Beyond Responding”

Everything above is reactive. Someone messages you, you respond.

The other half of the problem: relationships fade because no one reaches out at all. You think about someone, mean to text them, don’t, and six months later you’ve drifted apart. Not from conflict—from entropy.

The Weekly Relationship Review

Section titled “The Weekly Relationship Review”

Every Sunday, before clearing your backlog:

Your prompt:

The “Just Because” Message

Section titled “The “Just Because” Message”

The hardest messages to send are the ones with no agenda.

Your prompt:

Claude’s draft:

“Random thought—saw someone at the coffee shop reading that book you recommended and it made me smile. Hope you’re doing well.”

That message takes two seconds to send. Without the draft, I’d overthink it for an hour and never send it.

The Life Events Tracker

Section titled “The Life Events Tracker”

People tell you things. Their kid is starting school. They’re training for a marathon. They just moved. You forget.

With a notes MCP connected, Claude can help you track these:

Then monthly:

“How did your daughter’s first week of school go?” lands completely differently than generic check-ins.

Voice Calibration

Section titled “Voice Calibration”

Claude doesn’t know how you talk. But you can teach it.

Option 1: Show Examples

Section titled “Option 1: Show Examples”

Option 2: Describe Your Style

Section titled “Option 2: Describe Your Style”

Option 3: Iterate

Section titled “Option 3: Iterate”

After a few rounds, you’ll internalize what directions produce your voice.

What NOT to Do

Section titled “What NOT to Do”
  • Don’t auto-send anything. Claude drafts. You review. You send. The human judgment step isn’t optional.
  • Don’t use this for high-stakes emotional conversations. Breaking up with someone? Working through conflict with a family member? Have the actual conversation.
  • Don’t use it to be someone you’re not. If Claude’s draft sounds more articulate than you’d ever be, people will notice. Edit toward authenticity, not perfection.
  • Don’t over-explain. “Sorry this took so long, I had Claude help me draft this” is weird. Just send the message.

Limitations

Section titled “Limitations”
LimitationDescription
Nuanced relationshipsClaude doesn’t know your history. It can’t know that “sounds good” from your sister actually means she’s upset
Office politicsClaude doesn’t know that your “suggestion” to your boss needs to be framed differently than to your direct report
Cultural normsWhat’s friendly in one culture is overly casual in another. Claude defaults to American professional-casual
Platform constraintsClaude doesn’t know LinkedIn messages should be shorter than emails, or that paragraphs over Instagram DM seem intense
Truly difficult emotional situationsAI can help you find words. It can’t replace emotional intelligence

The Deeper Point

Section titled “The Deeper Point”

The goal isn’t to optimize communication. It’s to actually communicate.

The messages I avoided weren’t unimportant. They were from friends I cared about, clients I valued, colleagues I respected. The avoidance wasn’t laziness—it was friction accumulating until the activation energy felt insurmountable.

Claude addresses this by turning “I need to figure out what to say” into “I need to react to this draft.”

Since I started doing this:

  • Average response time dropped dramatically
  • The guilt backlog is gone
  • Relationships that were fading are active again
  • I enjoy messaging instead of dreading it
  • I’m proactively maintaining relationships I used to let drift

The tool isn’t the point. The relationships are. The tool just removes what was getting in the way.

Quick Start

Section titled “Quick Start”
  1. Open Claude Code
  2. Ask it to find your oldest unanswered iMessage:
  1. Draft a response:
  1. Review the draft
  2. Edit to your voice
  3. Send

One message. Today. Feel the relief.

Quick Reference

Section titled “Quick Reference”
PointContent
The insightReacting to a draft (recognition) is dramatically easier than creating from scratch (recall)
The power-upMCPs let Claude pull context from your calendar, email, notes, and other tools
The workflow (Mac)1. Ask Claude to read your iMessages 2. Tell Claude: context, tone, key point 3. Get draft 4. Edit for your voice 5. Send
The workflow (other platforms)1. Paste or screenshot the message 2. Tell Claude: context, tone, key point 3. Get draft 4. Edit for your voice 5. Send

Use it for:

  • Messages you’ve been avoiding
  • Professional emails requiring diplomacy
  • Networking follow-ups
  • Difficult workplace conversations
  • Emotionally sensitive messages
  • Scheduling logistics
  • Proactive outreach
  • Weekly backlog clearing

Don’t use it for:

  • Auto-sending without review
  • High-stakes emotional conversations
  • Pretending to be someone you’re not

Proactive system:

  • Weekly relationship review (Sundays)
  • “Just because” messages with no agenda
  • Life events follow-ups
  • Monthly connection audit

Voice calibration: Show examples, describe your style, or iterate (“too formal, make it casual”).

Useful MCP connections:

  • Calendar (real availability for scheduling)
  • Email (thread context)
  • Notes (your thinking, project details)
  • CRM (client history)
  • Slack (team context)

References

Section titled “References”
  • Original Twitter Thread: @ArmanHezarkhani
  • Claude Code Documentation: docs.anthropic.com
  • MCP Protocol: Model Context Protocol
Tags:
  • Claude Code
  • iMessage
  • AI Writing Assistant
  • Productivity Tools
  • Message Management
  • MCP

Coplay: AI Assistant for Unity Game Development - Supercharge Your Workflow

Jan 19, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

Game development is a complex, iterative process filled with repetitive tasks. Writing boilerplate code, setting up scenes, debugging scripts—these activities consume valuable time that could be spent on creative work.

Coplay is an AI assistant designed specifically for Unity game developers that lives directly inside the Unity Editor. Unlike generic AI coding tools, Coplay understands Unity’s architecture, components, and workflows deeply.

Why Coplay for Unity Development?

Section titled “Why Coplay for Unity Development?”

The Challenge of Traditional AI Tools

Section titled “The Challenge of Traditional AI Tools”

Most AI coding assistants operate at the code editor level, missing crucial context about your Unity project:

LimitationImpact
No Unity Editor awarenessCan’t manipulate scenes, prefabs, or game objects directly
Generic C# suggestionsDoesn’t understand Unity-specific patterns like coroutines, scriptable objects
Context switchingConstantly switching between IDE and browser for AI assistance

Coplay’s Unity-Native Advantage

Section titled “Coplay’s Unity-Native Advantage”

Coplay integrates as a C# plugin directly into Unity Editor, giving it:

  • Direct Scene Access: Manipulate GameObjects, components, and hierarchies
  • Unity Pattern Recognition: Understands MonoBehaviours, ScriptableObjects, serialization
  • Asset Pipeline Integration: Can work with prefabs, materials, and assets
  • Natural Language Interface: Describe what you want in plain English

Installation & Setup

Section titled “Installation & Setup”

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”
  • Unity 2022.3 or newer (LTS recommended)
  • Coplay supports both Unity Personal and Professional licenses

Installation Steps

Section titled “Installation Steps”

  1. Open Unity Package Manager

    • Window > Package Manager
    • Click the + icon
    • Select “Add package from git URL”

  2. Install Coplay Plugin

  3. Configure API Key

    • Open Edit > Preferences > Coplay
    • Enter your API key (sign up at coplay.dev)

Quick Start Workflow

Section titled “Quick Start Workflow”
  1. Open Coplay Panel: Window > Coplay
  2. Describe Your Task: “Create a player movement script with WASD controls”
  3. Review Suggestions: Coplay shows code you can preview
  4. Apply Changes: One-click to insert into your project

Core Features

Section titled “Core Features”

1. Code Generation

Section titled “1. Code Generation”

Generate Unity-specific scripts with proper patterns:

2. Scene Manipulation

Section titled “2. Scene Manipulation”

Modify your Unity hierarchy through natural language:

CommandAction
”Find all GameObjects with tag ‘Enemy‘“Returns filtered list
”Add a rigidbody to the player”Attaches component
”Create a cube at position (0, 2, 0)“Instantiates object
”Parent all coins to the coin manager”Reorganizes hierarchy

3. Debugging Assistant

Section titled “3. Debugging Assistant”

Describe your issue and get targeted help:

  • “My character falls through the floor”
  • “The animation isn’t playing when I press space”
  • “Fix the null reference in my inventory script”

Coplay analyzes your code and Unity console to provide solutions.

4. Asset Management

Section titled “4. Asset Management”

Work with Unity assets efficiently:

  • Create and configure ScriptableObjects
  • Generate material variants
  • Batch rename assets
  • Setup prefab variants

Pricing Model

Section titled “Pricing Model”

Coplay offers flexible pricing for different needs:

PlanPriceFeatures
Free$0Use your own API key, pay-as-you-go
ProfessionalFrom $29/moIncluded credits, priority support
StudioCustomTeam features, volume discounts

See coplay.dev/pricing for current details.

The free tier is perfect for:

  • Individual developers
  • Testing Coplay’s capabilities
  • Projects with variable AI needs

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Be Specific with Context

Section titled “1. Be Specific with Context”

Good: “Create a third-person camera controller that smooth-follows the player with adjustable distance and height, using Cinemachine if available”

Bad: “Make a camera script”

2. Iterate in Small Steps

Section titled “2. Iterate in Small Steps”

Break complex features into manageable pieces:

  1. First: “Create basic player movement”
  2. Then: “Add jumping with gravity”
  3. Then: “Add sprint mechanics”
  4. Finally: “Add camera follow”

3. Review Generated Code

Section titled “3. Review Generated Code”

Always review AI-generated code for:

  • Performance implications
  • Security concerns (networked games)
  • Team coding standards
  • Proper error handling

4. Leverage Unity Patterns

Section titled “4. Leverage Unity Patterns”

Ask Coplay to use established Unity patterns:

  • “Use ScriptableObject for game configuration”
  • “Implement a singleton GameManager”
  • “Use object pooling for projectiles”

Integration with Other Tools

Section titled “Integration with Other Tools”

Unity MCP vs Coplay

Section titled “Unity MCP vs Coplay”
FeatureCoplayUnity MCP
IntegrationNative Unity PluginMCP bridge for external AI
AI SourceBuilt-inClaude, Cursor, VS Code
Use CaseDirect Editor AIExternal IDE workflow

Both tools can complement each other in your workflow.

Real-World Examples

Section titled “Real-World Examples”

Example 1: Rapid Prototyping

Section titled “Example 1: Rapid Prototyping”

Request: “Create a simple platformer character controller with double jump, coyote time, and jump buffering”

Coplay generates a complete script with:

  • Buffered jump input (remembers jump press before landing)
  • Coyote time (allows jump shortly after leaving platform)
  • Double jump counter
  • Proper physics integration

Example 2: System Architecture

Section titled “Example 2: System Architecture”

Request: “Design an inventory system using ScriptableObjects for item definitions”

Coplay creates:

  • ItemData ScriptableObject base class
  • Inventory manager component
  • UI integration structure
  • Usage examples

Example 3: Bug Fixing

Section titled “Example 3: Bug Fixing”

Issue: “My enemy AI stops chasing after the player dies”

Coplay analyzes:

  • References to player GameObject
  • Null checks in chase logic
  • Event subscriptions/unsubscriptions
  • Provides the fix for proper cleanup

Community & Resources

Section titled “Community & Resources”
  • Official Website: coplay.dev
  • Documentation: docs.coplay.dev
  • GitHub: github.com/CoplayDev
  • YouTube Tutorial: Onboarding Guide
  • Game Jams: Regular Coplay Jams with prizes

Limitations & Considerations

Section titled “Limitations & Considerations”

What Coplay Does Well

Section titled “What Coplay Does Well”
  • Generating boilerplate Unity code
  • Exploring Unity APIs
  • Debugging common issues
  • Asset manipulation

When to Use Human Expertise

Section titled “When to Use Human Expertise”
  • Complex game design decisions
  • Performance optimization for large-scale games
  • Networked multiplayer architecture
  • Platform-specific optimizations

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Coplay represents a new category of AI tools: environment-aware assistants that understand the context of your specific development environment.

For Unity developers, Coplay isn’t just another coding assistant—it’s a collaborator that speaks your language, understands your tools, and helps you ship games faster.

The best way to understand Coplay’s value is to try it yourself. Install the plugin, describe your next tedious task, and watch hours of work turn into minutes.

References

Section titled “References”
  • Coplay Official Website
  • Coplay Documentation
  • Coplay GitHub Repository
  • Coplay Pricing
  • Unity MCP Comparison
  • GDC 2025 Talk: Speed Up Unity Game Development
Tags:
  • Unity
  • Game Development
  • AI Assistant
  • Coplay
  • MCP
  • C#
  • Productivity

Building an Automated Content Creation Workflow with Claude Code and Obsidian

Jan 5, 2026
Vibe Sparking
Tags:
  • claude-code
  • obsidian
  • mcp
  • productivity
  • knowledge-management
  • automation

Slidev AI: OpenMCP-Powered AI Presentation Generator Wins ModelScope Competition

Jan 5, 2026
Vibe Sparking
Tags:
  • AI
  • OpenMCP
  • Slidev
  • Presentation
  • ModelScope
  • MCP

Claude Code in Action: Official Course Review - Master Anthropic's AI CLI

Jan 4, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

In January 2026, Anthropic released Claude Code in Action — a comprehensive official course designed to teach developers how to harness the full power of Claude Code, their command-line AI assistant. Available on both Anthropic Skilljar and Coursera, this free course bridges the gap between basic AI assistance and professional-grade AI-powered development workflows.

“This course covers Claude Code, a command-line AI assistant that uses language models to perform development tasks.” — Anthropic

Course Overview

Section titled “Course Overview”

What You Get

Section titled “What You Get”
  • Format: Free online course with video content
  • Platform: Anthropic Skilljar / Coursera
  • Content: 15 lectures, approximately 1 hour of video content
  • Level: Beginner to intermediate developers
  • Prerequisites: Basic CLI familiarity and Claude Code access

Course Promise

Section titled “Course Promise”

The course promises to teach you how to:

  • Use Claude Code’s core tools for file manipulation, command execution, and code analysis
  • Manage context effectively using /init, CLAUDE.md files, and @ mentions
  • Control conversation flow with hotkeys and commands
  • Enable Plan Mode and Thinking Mode for complex tasks
  • Create custom commands for workflow automation
  • Extend functionality with MCP servers
  • Set up GitHub integration for PR reviews
  • Write custom hooks for behavior extension

Deep Dive: Course Content

Section titled “Deep Dive: Course Content”

1. Understanding Coding Assistants

Section titled “1. Understanding Coding Assistants”

The course begins by establishing a mental model for how AI coding assistants work:

  • How LLMs understand and manipulate code
  • The tool system architecture
  • Conversation management patterns
  • Context window considerations

2. Setup and Initial Configuration

Section titled “2. Setup and Initial Configuration”

Practical guidance on:

  • Installing Claude Code
  • API key configuration
  • Initial setup workflow
  • Understanding the CLI interface

3. Core Tools Mastery

Section titled “3. Core Tools Mastery”

File Operations:

  • Reading files with context awareness
  • Editing code with precision
  • Searching across codebases
  • Multi-file operations

Command Execution:

  • Running shell commands safely
  • Interpreting command output
  • Chaining operations
  • Error handling

Code Analysis:

  • Understanding code structure
  • Identifying patterns
  • Refactoring suggestions
  • Code review assistance

4. Context Management Strategies

Section titled “4. Context Management Strategies”

One of the most critical sections covers:

The /init Command:

  • Automatic project discovery
  • Generating project documentation
  • Creating knowledge bases

CLAUDE.md Files:

  • Project-specific instructions
  • Team conventions
  • Coding standards
  • Architecture documentation

@ Mentions:

  • Referencing files directly
  • Scoped context loading
  • Selective inclusion

5. Conversation Control

Section titled “5. Conversation Control”

Hotkeys and Commands:

  • Navigation shortcuts
  • Mode switching
  • Interruption and resumption
  • State management

6. Advanced Modes

Section titled “6. Advanced Modes”

Plan Mode:

  • When to use it
  • How it differs from normal operation
  • Best practices for planning sessions

Thinking Mode:

  • Deep analysis workflows
  • Complex problem-solving
  • Multi-step reasoning

7. Custom Commands

Section titled “7. Custom Commands”

Creating reusable workflow automation:

  • Command definition syntax
  • Parameter handling
  • Output formatting
  • Sharing commands across teams

8. MCP Servers Integration

Section titled “8. MCP Servers Integration”

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem:

  • What MCP servers are
  • Available community servers
  • Browser automation capabilities
  • Database integrations
  • API connections

Popular MCP Servers:

  • GitHub: PR reviews, issue management
  • Browser: Web automation, scraping
  • Database: Query interfaces
  • FileSystem: Extended file operations

9. GitHub Integration

Section titled “9. GitHub Integration”

Automating repository workflows:

  • Automated PR reviews
  • Issue triage
  • Commit message generation
  • Repository documentation

10. Hooks System

Section titled “10. Hooks System”

Extending Claude Code behavior:

  • PreToolUse: Actions before tool execution
  • PostToolUse: Actions after tool execution
  • UserPromptSubmit: Prompt processing
  • Stop: Cleanup on exit

Key Learnings and Insights

Section titled “Key Learnings and Insights”

1. Context is King

Section titled “1. Context is King”

The course emphasizes that effective context management is the single most important skill for using Claude Code productively. The /init command and CLAUDE.md files aren’t conveniences — they’re essential tools.

2. Start Simple, Scale Gradually

Section titled “2. Start Simple, Scale Gradually”

Beginners are encouraged to:

  • Start with basic file operations
  • Learn core hotkeys first
  • Gradually adopt advanced features
  • Build custom commands organically

3. MCP Changes Everything

Section titled “3. MCP Changes Everything”

The MCP ecosystem transforms Claude Code from a capable assistant into an extensible platform. The course provides just a taste of what’s possible with community servers.

4. Hooks Enable Workflow Integration

Section titled “4. Hooks Enable Workflow Integration”

For teams and power users, hooks are the secret sauce that makes Claude Code fit into existing development workflows seamlessly.

Comparison: Self-Taught vs. Course

Section titled “Comparison: Self-Taught vs. Course”
AspectSelf-TaughtAfter Course
Context ManagementBasic file readingStructured /init + CLAUDE.md
CommandsAd-hoc promptsReusable custom commands
IntegrationManual copy-pasteMCP + GitHub automation
WorkflowLinear conversationsHotkey-driven control
ExtensibilityUnknownHooks fully understood

Who Should Take This Course?

Section titled “Who Should Take This Course?”

Ideal For:

Section titled “Ideal For:”
  • Developers new to Claude Code
  • Teams adopting Claude Code
  • Users who feel they’re underutilizing the tool
  • Anyone wanting to build custom integrations

Maybe Not For:

Section titled “Maybe Not For:”
  • Advanced Claude Code users (may be too basic)
  • Those expecting deep LLM theory (this is practical)
  • Developers without CLI experience

Practical Takeaways

Section titled “Practical Takeaways”

Immediate Wins

Section titled “Immediate Wins”

After completing the course, you can immediately:

  1. Set up proper project contexts — Stop pasting code, use /init
  2. Create custom commands — Automate repetitive workflows
  3. Use hotkeys effectively — Control conversations fluidly
  4. Add an MCP server — Extend capabilities
  5. Write a simple hook — Customize behavior

Recommended Next Steps

Section titled “Recommended Next Steps”
  1. Practice daily — Use Claude Code for real work
  2. Explore MCP servers — Check MCPmarket
  3. Join the community — Reddit r/ClaudeAI
  4. Build custom commands — Share with your team
  5. Experiment with hooks — Tailor to your workflow

Limitations and Gaps

Section titled “Limitations and Gaps”

What the Course Doesn’t Cover

Section titled “What the Course Doesn’t Cover”
  • Advanced prompting techniques — Barely touches on prompt engineering
  • Multi-agent orchestration — No coverage of agent systems
  • Enterprise deployment — Team scaling strategies absent
  • Performance optimization — Token management basics only
  • Advanced MCP patterns — Server creation not covered

Complementary Resources

Section titled “Complementary Resources”

To fill these gaps, consider:

  • Claude Code CLI: The Definitive Technical Reference — Deep technical coverage
  • MCPcat — MCP server guide
  • Community repositories — Real-world examples and configs

Real-World Application

Section titled “Real-World Application”

A Sample Workflow After Taking the Course

Section titled “A Sample Workflow After Taking the Course”

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Claude Code in Action is a well-structured, practical introduction to Anthropic’s AI coding assistant. It won’t make you an expert overnight, but it provides the foundation needed to use Claude Code effectively in professional development workflows.

Value Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (4/5)

  • Beginners: Essential starting point
  • Intermediate Users: Good for filling knowledge gaps
  • Experts: May find it basic

The course is free, takes about an hour, and delivers tangible value. If you’re using Claude Code but feel like you’re missing features, this course will likely unlock capabilities you didn’t know existed.

“The best way to learn Claude Code is to use Claude Code to learn Claude Code.” — Course Philosophy

Sources:

  • Claude Code in Action - Anthropic Skilljar
  • Claude Code in Action - Coursera
  • Claude Code MCP Documentation
  • Claude Code CLI Comprehensive Guide
  • MCPcat Claude Code Guide
  • AI Learning Resources - Anthropic
  • Reddit Discussion
Tags:
  • AI
  • Claude Code
  • Anthropic
  • Developer Tools
  • CLI
  • Course Review
  • MCP
  • GitHub Integration

Eigent AI: The World's First Multi-Agent Workforce Platform

Jan 4, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Eigent AI: The World’s First Multi-Agent Workforce Platform

Section titled “Eigent AI: The World’s First Multi-Agent Workforce Platform”

Introduction: The New Productivity Engine

Section titled “Introduction: The New Productivity Engine”

In the rapidly evolving landscape of AI automation, Eigent emerges as the world’s first Multi-Agent Workforce desktop application. Built on the robust foundation of CAMEL-AI, Eigent empowers users to build, manage, and deploy a custom AI workforce that transforms complex workflows into automated tasks.

Build your own AI Agent Workforce with Eigent — a non-intrusive integration that offers adaptable, ever-improving performance for any scenario, letting AI Workers run your operations automatically.

Key Features

Section titled “Key Features”

🏭 Multi-Agent Workforce

Section titled “🏭 Multi-Agent Workforce”

Eigent employs a team of specialized AI agents that collaborate to solve complex tasks. The platform dynamically breaks down tasks and activates multiple agents to work in parallel, significantly boosting productivity through coordinated effort.

Pre-defined Agent Workers:

  • Developer Agent: Writes and executes code, runs terminal commands
  • Search Agent: Searches the web and extracts content
  • Document Agent: Creates and manages documents
  • Multi-Modal Agent: Processes images and audio

🔌 MCP Tools Integration

Section titled “🔌 MCP Tools Integration”

Eigent comes with massive built-in Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools and allows you to install your own custom tools. Equip agents with exactly the right tools for your scenarios — even integrate internal APIs or custom functions.

Built-in MCP Tools Include:

  • Web browsing capabilities
  • Code execution environments
  • Notion integration
  • Google Suite connectivity
  • Slack integration
  • And many more…

🎯 Infinitely Customizable

Section titled “🎯 Infinitely Customizable”

Build your own worker nodes to execute any task. With pluggable MCPs, you have full control to create agents with the perfect tools for the job. Your workforce, your rules.

🔒 Private and Secure by Design

Section titled “🔒 Private and Secure by Design”

Your data stays yours, always. With a focus on local, private deployment, your workflows and sensitive information never leave your control. Eigent is open-source for full transparency and trust.

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”

☁️ Cloud Version

Section titled “☁️ Cloud Version”

The fastest way to experience Eigent’s multi-agent AI capabilities is through their cloud platform:

  • Instant Access - Start building multi-agent workflows in minutes
  • Managed Infrastructure - They handle scaling, updates, and maintenance
  • Premium Support - Subscribe and get priority assistance from the engineering team

Get started at Eigent.ai →

🏠 Self-Hosting (Community Edition)

Section titled “🏠 Self-Hosting (Community Edition)”

For users who prefer local control, data privacy, or customization:

Prerequisites:

  • Node.js (version 18-22) and npm

Benefits of Self-Hosting:

  • Data Privacy - Keep sensitive data within your infrastructure
  • Customization - Modify and extend the platform to fit your needs
  • Cost Control - Avoid recurring cloud fees for large-scale deployments

🏢 Enterprise

Section titled “🏢 Enterprise”

For organizations requiring maximum security, customization, and control:

  • Commercial License available
  • Exclusive Features (like SSO & custom development)
  • Scalable Enterprise Deployment
  • Negotiated SLAs & implementation services

Contact: info@eigent.ai

Real-World Use Cases

Section titled “Real-World Use Cases”

1. Travel Planning

Section titled “1. Travel Planning”

Prompt: “We are two tennis fans and want to go see the tennis tournament in Palm Springs 2026. I live in SF - please prepare a detailed itinerary with flights, hotels, things to do for 3 days - around the time semifinal/finals are happening. We like hiking, vegan food and spas. Our budget is $5K.”

Eigent successfully planned a detailed 3-day tennis trip from SF to Palm Springs with flights, hotels, vegan food, hiking, spas, and finals tickets — all within a $5K budget, ending with a report + Slack message.

2. Financial Reporting

Section titled “2. Financial Reporting”

Prompt: “Please help me prepare a Q2 financial statement based on my bank transfer record file bank_transaction.csv in my desktop to a html report with chart to investors how much we have spent.”

Eigent processed CSV bank data and created an investor-ready HTML report with Q2 spending summary and charts.

3. Market Research

Section titled “3. Market Research”

Prompt: “Analyze the UK healthcare industry to support the planning of my next company. Provide a comprehensive market overview, including current trends, growth projections, and relevant regulations.”

Eigent delivered comprehensive market analysis with trends, regulations, growth projections, and identified top opportunities in professional HTML format.

4. Social Media Automation

Section titled “4. Social Media Automation”

Using customized Twitter MCP and Twitter Agent powered by open-source self-deployed models to help get information from public online posts then send tweets automatically.

5. Data Extraction

Section titled “5. Data Extraction”

Eigent collected all stargazers of the eigent-ai/eigent repository and built a table containing each stargazer’s GitHub username, name, GitHub profile URL, and public LinkedIn and X (Twitter) links found on their GitHub profile pages.

Tech Stack

Section titled “Tech Stack”

Backend

Section titled “Backend”
  • Framework: FastAPI
  • Package Manager: uv
  • Async Server: Uvicorn
  • Authentication: OAuth 2.0, Passlib
  • Multi-agent framework: CAMEL

Frontend

Section titled “Frontend”
  • Framework: React
  • Desktop App Framework: Electron
  • Language: TypeScript
  • UI: Tailwind CSS, Radix UI, Lucide React, Framer Motion
  • State Management: Zustand
  • Flow Editor: React Flow

Development Roadmap

Section titled “Development Roadmap”
TopicsDescriptionDiscord Channel
Context EngineeringPrompt caching, System prompt optimize, Toolkit docstring optimize, Context compressionJoin Discord
Multi-modal EnhancementMore accurate image understanding when using browser, Advanced video generationJoin Discord
Multi-agent systemWorkforce support fixed workflow, Workforce support multi-round conversionJoin Discord
Browser ToolkitBrowseCamp integration, Benchmark improvement, Forbid repeated page visiting, Automatic cache button clickingJoin Discord
Document ToolkitSupport dynamic file editingJoin Discord
Terminal ToolkitBenchmark improvement, Terminal-Bench integrationJoin Discord
Environment & RLEnvironment design, Data-generation, RL framework integration (VERL, TRL, OpenRLHF)Join Discord

Why Choose Eigent?

Section titled “Why Choose Eigent?”

✅ Zero Setup

Section titled “✅ Zero Setup”

No technical configuration required — download and start building your AI workforce.

✅ Multi-Agent Coordination

Section titled “✅ Multi-Agent Coordination”

Handle complex multi-agent workflows with intelligent task distribution.

✅ Enterprise Features

Section titled “✅ Enterprise Features”

SSO/Access control for organizational deployment.

✅ Local Deployment

Section titled “✅ Local Deployment”

Keep your data private with full local deployment capabilities.

✅ 100% Open Source

Section titled “✅ 100% Open Source”

Full transparency — download, inspect, and modify the code.

✅ Custom Model Support

Section titled “✅ Custom Model Support”

Deploy with your preferred models, whether cloud or local.

✅ MCP Integration

Section titled “✅ MCP Integration”

Massive ecosystem of tools with custom tool installation support.

Community & Support

Section titled “Community & Support”
  • GitHub: eigent-ai/eigent — Report bugs, request features, and track development
  • Discord: Join Discord — Get real-time support, chat with the community
  • X (Twitter): @Eigent_AI — Follow for updates, AI insights, and announcements
  • Documentation: docs.eigent.ai — Comprehensive guides and API reference

Licensing

Section titled “Licensing”

Eigent is licensed under the Eigent Open Source License, based on Apache 2.0 with additional conditions. For enterprise licensing inquiries, contact info@eigent.ai.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Eigent represents a paradigm shift in AI automation — moving beyond single-agent chatbots to a true multi-agent workforce platform. Whether you’re an individual looking to automate personal workflows or an enterprise seeking scalable AI solutions, Eigent provides the tools, flexibility, and security to build your perfect AI workforce.

Liked this article? Follow for more insights on AI automation, multi-agent systems, and the future of work.

References:

  • Eigent Official Website
  • Eigent GitHub Repository
  • CAMEL-AI Framework
