Desktop Commander MCP: Terminal Control & File System Access for Claude

Dec 22, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Desktop Commander MCP: Terminal Control & File System Access for Claude

Section titled “Desktop Commander MCP: Terminal Control & File System Access for Claude”

In the rapidly evolving AI development landscape, Desktop Commander MCP stands out as a game-changing tool that transforms Claude Desktop into a powerful desktop automation assistant. Unlike traditional IDE-focused tools, Desktop Commander provides a solution-centric approach that works with your entire operating system.

What is Desktop Commander MCP?

Section titled “What is Desktop Commander MCP?”

Desktop Commander MCP is an open-source MCP (Model Context Protocol) server created by wonderwhy-er that enables Claude to:

  • Execute terminal commands with output streaming
  • Search and manage file systems
  • Edit files with surgical precision using diff capabilities
  • Read and write Excel, PDF, and various data files
  • Run code in memory without file persistence

“Work with code and text, run processes and automate tasks beyond typical AI editor limitations.”

Key Features & Highlights

Section titled “Key Features & Highlights”

1. Terminal & Process Control

Section titled “1. Terminal & Process Control”

Desktop Commander provides comprehensive terminal management:

Capabilities include:

  • Command execution with configurable timeouts
  • Background process execution
  • Process listing and termination
  • Session management for SSH, databases, and development servers

2. Native Data File Support

Section titled “2. Native Data File Support”

One of Desktop Commander’s standout features is native file handling:

Excel Support (.xlsx, .xls, .xlsm):

  • Read, write, and edit spreadsheets
  • Search within Excel files
  • No external tools required

PDF Support:

  • Text extraction from PDF documents
  • PDF creation from markdown
  • Document modification capabilities

Data Analysis:

  • Instant CSV/JSON file analysis
  • Quick data exploration without setup

3. Advanced File Operations

Section titled “3. Advanced File Operations”

4. Surgical Code Editing

Section titled “4. Surgical Code Editing”

Desktop Commander offers two editing approaches:

  1. Targeted Replacements - For precise, surgical changes
  2. Full File Rewrites - For major modifications

Multi-file support with pattern-based replacements makes refactoring efficient.

5. In-Memory Code Execution

Section titled “5. In-Memory Code Execution”

Execute code without file persistence:

  • Python - Run Python scripts directly
  • Node.js - Execute JavaScript/TypeScript
  • R - Statistical computing support

6. Configuration Management

Section titled “6. Configuration Management”
  • Dynamic get/set configuration values
  • Batch settings updates
  • Changes without server restart

7. Audit Logging

Section titled “7. Audit Logging”
  • Automatic logging of all tool calls
  • 10MB log rotation limit
  • Detailed timestamps and arguments

Installation Options

Section titled “Installation Options”

Option 1: NPX (⭐ Recommended - Auto-Updates)

Section titled “Option 1: NPX (⭐ Recommended - Auto-Updates)”

Option 2: Bash Installer (macOS)

Section titled “Option 2: Bash Installer (macOS)”

Option 3: Smithery

Section titled “Option 3: Smithery”

Visit smithery.ai/server/@wonderwhy-er/desktop-commander

Option 4: Manual Configuration

Section titled “Option 4: Manual Configuration”

Add to claude_desktop_config.json:

Config file locations:

  • macOS: ~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json
  • Windows: %APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json
  • Linux: ~/.config/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json

Option 5: Local Checkout

Section titled “Option 5: Local Checkout”

Option 6: Docker 🐳 (No Node.js Required)

Section titled “Option 6: Docker 🐳 (No Node.js Required)”

Docker Configuration:

Recommended Scenarios & Best Practices

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios & Best Practices”

1. Development Workflow Automation

Section titled “1. Development Workflow Automation”

Real user feedback:

“I had 76 errors in 23 files in my Svelte 5 project. Used desktop-commander, sequentialthinking, and tree-sitter to fix them all. Never resolved type errors this quickly with AI before!“

2. Data Analysis Pipeline

Section titled “2. Data Analysis Pipeline”

3. System Administration

Section titled “3. System Administration”

4. Codebase Exploration

Section titled “4. Codebase Exploration”

5. Sandboxed Development (Docker)

Section titled “5. Sandboxed Development (Docker)”

For enhanced security, use Docker isolation:

  • Controlled environment with persistent development state
  • Selective folder access without full filesystem exposure
  • Installed tools and configurations persist across sessions

Daily Workflow Examples

Section titled “Daily Workflow Examples”

Morning Code Review

Section titled “Morning Code Review”

Documentation Update

Section titled “Documentation Update”

Database Management

Section titled “Database Management”

File Organization

Section titled “File Organization”

Precautions & Security

Section titled “Precautions & Security”

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”
  1. Use Docker for Isolation - Provides controlled environment
  2. Configure Command Blacklists - Restrict dangerous commands
  3. Set Directory Permissions - Limit access to specific folders
  4. Review Audit Logs - Monitor all executed commands
  5. Backup Before Operations - Especially for destructive tasks

Security Controls

Section titled “Security Controls”

Desktop Commander provides:

  • Command blacklist configuration
  • Directory access permissions
  • Audit logging for all operations
  • Telemetry opt-out options

Comparison with Similar Tools

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Tools”
FeatureDesktop CommanderMCP Filesystem ServerIDE Extensions
Terminal Control✅ Full❌ NoneLimited
File Search✅ RipgrepBasicVaries
Excel Support✅ Native❌ NonePlugin needed
PDF Support✅ Native❌ NonePlugin needed
Diff Editing✅ SurgicalBasic✅ Yes
Code Execution✅ In-memory❌ NoneVaries
Process Management✅ Full❌ NoneLimited
Docker Support✅ Yes❌ NoN/A
Auto-Updates✅ Options 1-4, 6ManualVia store

Recent Updates (2025)

Section titled “Recent Updates (2025)”
  • v0.1.40 (May 20) - Audit logging, improved line-based operations, enhanced edit_block
  • Fuzzy Search Logging (May 5) - Comprehensive logging for search operations
  • Telemetry Opt-out (April 29) - Configuration-based telemetry control
  • Better Code Search (March 25) - Enhanced context-aware results
  • WSL Support - Windows Subsystem for Linux exploration

Troubleshooting & FAQ

Section titled “Troubleshooting & FAQ”

Q: Commands are timing out

Section titled “Q: Commands are timing out”

A: Increase timeout in configuration or use background execution for long-running tasks.

Q: Permission denied errors

Section titled “Q: Permission denied errors”

A: Check directory access permissions in Desktop Commander configuration.

Q: Docker container not persisting data

Section titled “Q: Docker container not persisting data”

A: Use volume mounts (-v flag) for data persistence.

Q: Can’t find files with search

Section titled “Q: Can’t find files with search”

A: Ensure the search path is within allowed directories and try adjusting the search pattern.

Q: Updates not applying

Section titled “Q: Updates not applying”

A: For Options 1-4 and 6, restart Claude. For Option 5, run git pull && npm run setup.

Uninstallation

Section titled “Uninstallation”

Automatic (Recommended)

Section titled “Automatic (Recommended)”

Manual

Section titled “Manual”

Edit claude_desktop_config.json and remove the “desktop-commander” entry from mcpServers.

Community & Support

Section titled “Community & Support”
  • Website: desktopcommander.app
  • GitHub: wonderwhy-er/DesktopCommanderMCP
  • Discord: Join the Community
  • License: MIT (Free and Open Source)

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Desktop Commander MCP represents a paradigm shift in how we interact with AI assistants for desktop automation. By providing Claude with comprehensive terminal control, file system access, and native data file support, it transforms Claude from a conversational AI into a powerful automation partner.

Whether you’re a developer looking to streamline your workflow, a data analyst needing quick insights, or a system administrator automating routine tasks, Desktop Commander MCP offers the tools you need. Its active development, growing community, and commitment to open-source principles make it an essential addition to any Claude Desktop setup.

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • Desktop Commander Official Website
  • GitHub Repository
  • Smithery Server Page
  • Discord Community
  • MCP Protocol Documentation
  • Claude Desktop Setup Guide
Tags:
  • AI
  • MCP
  • Claude
  • Terminal
  • Automation
  • Desktop Commander
  • File System
  • Developer Tools

Desktop Commander MCP: Transform Claude into Your Ultimate AI Development Partner

Dec 22, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Desktop Commander MCP: Transform Claude into Your Ultimate AI Development Partner

Section titled “Desktop Commander MCP: Transform Claude into Your Ultimate AI Development Partner”

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

What if your AI assistant could not only understand your code but also execute commands, manage files, and work across your entire operating system? Desktop Commander MCP makes this possible, transforming Claude Desktop into a powerful, autonomous development partner that goes far beyond traditional IDE-focused AI tools.

Created by Eduard Ruzga (wonderwhy-er), Desktop Commander MCP is a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that bridges Claude Desktop with your local system. With over thousands of weekly npm downloads and a growing community of enthusiastic developers, it’s rapidly becoming the go-to solution for developers who want AI-assisted development without the fragmentation of multiple tools.

Key Features and Highlights

Section titled “Key Features and Highlights”

🚀 Enhanced Terminal Control

Section titled “🚀 Enhanced Terminal Control”

Desktop Commander provides intelligent terminal integration with:

  • Smart process detection - Automatically knows when programs are ready for input
  • Interactive session management - Maintain long-running sessions (SSH, databases, dev servers)
  • Background execution - Run commands with timeout support
  • Process control - List, monitor, and terminate processes

📁 Comprehensive File Operations

Section titled “📁 Comprehensive File Operations”
  • Full filesystem access with intelligent permissions
  • Native Excel support (.xlsx, .xls, .xlsm) - read, write, edit, search
  • PDF capabilities - Extract text, create from markdown, modify existing PDFs
  • Surgical code editing - Search/replace blocks for precise changes
  • Pattern-based replacements with vscode-ripgrep integration
  • URL support - Fetch content from web resources directly

💻 In-Memory Code Execution

Section titled “💻 In-Memory Code Execution”

Execute code without saving files:

🔧 Configuration Management

Section titled “🔧 Configuration Management”

Dynamic runtime configuration:

  • Blocked commands for security
  • Shell preferences (bash, zsh, PowerShell)
  • Directory access controls
  • File read/write limits

Prerequisites and Installation

Section titled “Prerequisites and Installation”

System Requirements

Section titled “System Requirements”
  • Node.js (v16 or higher recommended)
  • Claude Desktop application
  • Operating System: macOS, Windows, or Linux

Installation Options

Section titled “Installation Options”

Option 1: NPX (Recommended - Auto-Updates)

Section titled “Option 1: NPX (Recommended - Auto-Updates)”

Option 2: macOS Bash Script

Section titled “Option 2: macOS Bash Script”

Option 3: Docker (Complete Isolation)

Section titled “Option 3: Docker (Complete Isolation)”

Option 4: Manual Configuration

Section titled “Option 4: Manual Configuration”

Add to your claude_desktop_config.json:

Config locations:

  • macOS: ~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json
  • Windows: %APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json
  • Linux: ~/.config/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json

Recommended Scenarios and Daily Workflow

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios and Daily Workflow”

1. Codebase Exploration and Documentation

Section titled “1. Codebase Exploration and Documentation”

2. Multi-Project Development

Section titled “2. Multi-Project Development”

Unlike IDE tools limited to one workspace, Claude with Desktop Commander can:

  • Work across multiple repositories simultaneously
  • Compare implementations between projects
  • Copy patterns from one project to another

3. Automated DevOps Tasks

Section titled “3. Automated DevOps Tasks”

4. Data Analysis

Section titled “4. Data Analysis”

5. Interactive Development Environments

Section titled “5. Interactive Development Environments”

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

Security-First Configuration

Section titled “Security-First Configuration”
  1. Use dedicated configuration chats - Make config changes in separate conversations
  2. Set specific allowedDirectories - Limit filesystem access to needed paths
  3. Review blockedCommands - Prevent dangerous command execution
  4. Consider Docker for sensitive work - Complete isolation from host system

Efficient File Editing

Section titled “Efficient File Editing”

The edit_block tool uses search/replace blocks for surgical edits:

Pro Tips:

  • Make multiple small edits instead of large rewrites
  • Keep fileWriteLineLimit small to encourage efficient behavior
  • Use fuzzy search logs for debugging match failures

Session Management

Section titled “Session Management”

For long-running operations:

  1. Use start_process with smart ready detection
  2. Interact via interact_with_process
  3. Monitor with read_process_output
  4. Clean up with force_terminate

Comparison with Similar Tools

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Tools”
FeatureDesktop CommanderCursorWindsurf
ScopeEntire OSSingle projectSingle project
File ReadingFull filesChunkedChunked
Multi-Project✅ Yes❌ No❌ No
Terminal Control✅ Interactive⚠️ Limited⚠️ Limited
Cost ModelPro subscriptionAPI tokensAPI tokens
Token EconomyEfficientCan cascadeCan cascade

Why Developers Are Switching

Section titled “Why Developers Are Switching”

From the community testimonials:

“It’s a life saver! I paid for Claude + Cursor which always felt duplicated. This solves the problem ultimately.”

“Claude first to keep my sanity in check, then if necessary, engage with other IDEs.”

“I was able to compare old and new codebase fully, accounting for updates in the code, and fix issues in hours that I’d spent days on with other tools.”

Precautions and Troubleshooting

Section titled “Precautions and Troubleshooting”

Known Limitations

Section titled “Known Limitations”
  1. Directory restrictions can be bypassed - Security through allowedDirectories is not absolute
  2. Terminal commands unrestricted - allowedDirectories only affects file operations
  3. File write limits - Default 50 lines per write to encourage efficient AI behavior

Common Issues

Section titled “Common Issues”

Claude times out:

  • Ensure MCP server is running
  • Check config JSON syntax
  • Restart Claude Desktop

Commands fail:

  • Verify shell path in configuration
  • Check command isn’t in blockedCommands
  • Ensure proper permissions

File access denied:

  • Add path to allowedDirectories
  • Verify file permissions on disk

Debug Mode

Section titled “Debug Mode”

For troubleshooting:

Connect debugger to Node.js process on port 9229.

FAQ

Section titled “FAQ”

Q: Do I need API credits? A: No. Works with Claude Desktop Pro subscription ($20/month), not API calls.

Q: Does it auto-update? A: Yes, when installed via npx or Smithery, updates apply on Claude restart.

Q: Is my data collected? A: Limited pseudonymous telemetry only. No file contents or paths. Opt-out available.

Q: Can I use it with other Claude clients? A: Designed for Claude Desktop. Community adapters may exist for other clients.

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • GitHub Repository: wonderwhy-er/DesktopCommanderMCP
  • NPM Package: @wonderwhy-er/desktop-commander
  • Official Website: desktopcommander.app
  • Discord Community: Join Server
  • Video Tutorial: YouTube Setup Guide
  • Author’s Article: Claude with MCPs Replaced Cursor & Windsurf

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Desktop Commander MCP represents a paradigm shift in AI-assisted development. By giving Claude direct access to your terminal and filesystem, it transforms conversational AI into an active development partner. Whether you’re exploring unfamiliar codebases, automating DevOps tasks, or building new features, Desktop Commander puts all your development tools in one intelligent chat interface.

The solution-centric approach—working with your entire OS rather than a single IDE workspace—combined with the cost efficiency of subscription-based pricing, makes it an compelling alternative to fragmented, API-token-hungry alternatives.

Ready to supercharge your development workflow? Install Desktop Commander and experience the future of AI-assisted development today.

Tags:
  • MCP
  • Claude
  • AI
  • Desktop Commander
  • Automation
  • Development
  • Terminal
  • File Management

A2UI: Google's Agent-Driven Interface Protocol That Changes Everything

Dec 21, 2025
Vibe Sparking

From Text-Only Responses to Rich, Native UIs: How Google’s A2UI Protocol Enables AI Agents to Generate Interactive Interfaces

1. Introduction: Features and Highlights

Section titled “1. Introduction: Features and Highlights”

What is A2UI?

Section titled “What is A2UI?”

On December 15, 2025, Google publicly launched A2UI (Agent to UI), an open-source protocol that enables AI agents to generate rich, interactive user interfaces that render natively across platforms—without executing arbitrary code.

Think about it: instead of an AI agent responding with just text, it can now send back a complete, interactive form, a data visualization card, or a booking confirmation interface. The magic? Your app renders these UIs using native components, maintaining your design system while benefiting from AI-generated interfaces.

Core Problem Solved

Section titled “Core Problem Solved”

Traditional agent interactions face a fundamental tension:

ApproachProblem
Text-only responsesLimited expressiveness, tedious back-and-forth
HTML/JavaScript in iframesSecurity risks, sandboxing complexity, no native styling
A2UI approachDeclarative JSON data + native rendering = best of both worlds

Key Features

Section titled “Key Features”
FeatureDescription
Security-FirstDeclarative data format (not executable code). Agents can only render pre-approved components from your catalog
LLM-FriendlyFlat component lists with ID references, easy for LLMs to generate incrementally
Framework-AgnosticWorks with React, Flutter, Angular, SwiftUI, Lit, and more
Transport-AgnosticCompatible with A2A, AG-UI, REST (experimental)
Progressive RenderingUsers see UI building in real-time as LLMs generate

Who’s Behind It?

Section titled “Who’s Behind It?”
  • Core Team: Google A2UI Team
  • Key Partners: CopilotKit/AG-UI (day-zero compatibility), Opal (core contributor), Gemini Enterprise
  • License: Apache 2.0
  • Current Version: v0.8 (Public Preview)

2. Prerequisites, Installation and Configuration

Section titled “2. Prerequisites, Installation and Configuration”

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”

Before getting started, ensure you have:

  • Node.js (for web clients and renderers)
  • Python 3.8+ (for agent samples)
  • Gemini API Key (for demo agents)
  • UV package manager (recommended for Python)

Quick Installation

Section titled “Quick Installation”

Clone the repository:

Set up your API key:

Running the Restaurant Finder Demo

Section titled “Running the Restaurant Finder Demo”

This demo showcases A2UI’s full capabilities with a Gemini-powered agent:

1. Start the backend agent:

2. Start the web client (Lit renderer):

3. Open your browser to http://localhost:5173 and start chatting!

Project Structure

Section titled “Project Structure”

Supported Renderers

Section titled “Supported Renderers”
FrameworkStatusNotes
LitAvailableDemonstrated in samples
AngularAvailableWeb components integration
FlutterAvailableGenUI SDK uses A2UI internally
ReactRoadmapPriority for v1.0
SwiftUIRoadmapiOS/macOS native
Jetpack ComposeRoadmapAndroid native

3. Recommended Scenarios and Best Practices

Section titled “3. Recommended Scenarios and Best Practices”

Best Use Cases

Section titled “Best Use Cases”

1. Dynamic Form Generation

Section titled “1. Dynamic Form Generation”

Perfect for context-specific forms that adapt to user needs:

2. Multi-Agent Collaboration

Section titled “2. Multi-Agent Collaboration”

Remote agents can generate UIs within orchestrator contexts:

  • Agent A handles data retrieval
  • Agent B generates visualization cards
  • Orchestrator combines UIs seamlessly

3. Enterprise Automation

Section titled “3. Enterprise Automation”
  • Custom workflow dashboards
  • Approval interfaces
  • Real-time status visualizations

4. Cross-Platform Consistency

Section titled “4. Cross-Platform Consistency”

Same agent response renders natively on:

  • Web browsers
  • Mobile apps (iOS/Android)
  • Desktop applications

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Define Your Component Catalog

Start with a curated set of trusted components:

2. Keep Components Simple

Each component should have a single responsibility. Compose complex UIs from simple building blocks.

3. Use Data Binding Effectively

Separate UI structure from data model:

4. Handle Incremental Updates

Leverage A2UI’s flat structure for efficient partial updates:

Daily Workflow Examples

Section titled “Daily Workflow Examples”

Work Scenario - Project Status Dashboard:

Life Scenario - Restaurant Discovery:

4. Precautions, FAQ, and Troubleshooting

Section titled “4. Precautions, FAQ, and Troubleshooting”

Important Precautions

Section titled “Important Precautions”
AreaRecommendation
SecurityOnly include components in your catalog that you trust. A2UI is declarative, not executable, but your renderer implementations must be secure
VersioningA2UI is v0.8 (Public Preview). Expect breaking changes before v1.0
PerformanceFor complex UIs, consider pagination and lazy loading in your renderer
AccessibilityYour renderer is responsible for accessibility. A2UI provides structure; you provide semantics

FAQ

Section titled “FAQ”

Q: How is A2UI different from sending HTML?

A: A2UI sends structured JSON that describes UI components. Your app renders these using native widgets, maintaining your design system, accessibility, and security. HTML/JS requires sandboxing and can’t integrate with native styling.

Q: Can agents execute arbitrary code through A2UI?

A: No. A2UI is a declarative data format. Agents can only request components from your pre-approved catalog. Your renderer decides how to present them.

Q: Does A2UI replace MCP (Model Context Protocol)?

A: No, they’re complementary. MCP connects agents to tools and data. A2UI defines how agents describe UIs. You can use both in the same system.

Q: What about AG-UI?

A: AG-UI is the transport layer for real-time agent-UI communication. A2UI defines the payload format. They work together beautifully.

Q: Is A2UI production-ready?

A: A2UI is v0.8 (Public Preview). It’s functional but evolving. Production use should plan for specification updates.

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

Problem: Agent responses not rendering

Solution: Ensure your agent’s output matches the A2UI schema with proper delimiters.

Problem: Components not appearing

Solution: Verify the component type is in your catalog:

Problem: Data binding not updating

Solution: Check that your data model is properly initialized and the bind paths are correct:

5. Collaboration with Similar Products and Comparative Analysis

Section titled “5. Collaboration with Similar Products and Comparative Analysis”

The Agentic UI Protocol Landscape

Section titled “The Agentic UI Protocol Landscape”

In 2025, three complementary protocols have emerged as the foundation for agent-driven interfaces:

ProtocolPurposeCreatorFocus
A2UIDeclarative UI descriptionsGoogleWhat UI to render (payload)
MCPAgent-to-tool connectionsAnthropicHow agents access tools (vertical)
AG-UIReal-time agent-UI communicationCopilotKitHow UIs receive updates (transport)
A2AAgent-to-agent communicationGoogleHow agents talk to each other (horizontal)

A2UI vs MCP-UI Comparison

Section titled “A2UI vs MCP-UI Comparison”
AspectA2UIMCP-UI/MCP Apps
PhilosophyNative-first: “Blueprint” for native componentsWeb-first: HTML/JS in sandboxed iframes
RenderingApp renders using native widgetsBrowser renders sandboxed web content
StylingInherits host app stylingIsolated styling in sandbox
Security ModelComponent catalog restrictionIframe sandboxing
Cross-PlatformSame payload works on web, mobile, desktopPrimarily web-focused
Backed ByGoogleAnthropic, OpenAI

How They Work Together

Section titled “How They Work Together”

Comparative Analysis with Opal

Section titled “Comparative Analysis with Opal”

Opal (also from Google) uses A2UI as its underlying protocol:

  • Opal provides higher-level abstractions for AI mini-apps
  • A2UI provides the wire protocol for UI descriptions
  • Together they enable “one-sentence AI apps”

When to Use What

Section titled “When to Use What”
ScenarioRecommended Protocol
Native mobile app with AI featuresA2UI + AG-UI
Web chat with rich responsesMCP Apps or A2UI
Multi-agent orchestration with UIA2UI + A2A + AG-UI
Tool-calling with data accessMCP
Cross-platform consistencyA2UI

6. Reference Links

Section titled “6. Reference Links”

Official Resources

Section titled “Official Resources”
ResourceURL
A2UI Official Sitea2ui.org
GitHub Repositorygithub.com/google/A2UI
Google Developers Blog AnnouncementIntroducing A2UI
A2UI Composer (Widget Builder)go.copilotkit.ai/A2UI-widget-builder

Documentation

Section titled “Documentation”
TopicLink
What is A2UIa2ui.org/introduction/what-is-a2ui
Core Conceptsa2ui.org/core-concepts
Specificationa2ui.org/specification

Related Protocols

Section titled “Related Protocols”
ProtocolDocumentation
MCP (Model Context Protocol)modelcontextprotocol.io
A2A (Agent-to-Agent)a2aprotocol.ai
AG-UIcopilotkit.ai/ag-ui

Community & Learning

Section titled “Community & Learning”
ResourceDescription
CopilotKit BlogThe State of Agentic UI
DEV.to TutorialA2UI in Practice
Hacker News DiscussionA2UI Protocol Discussion

Roadmap

Section titled “Roadmap”

The A2UI team has prioritized:

  1. Specification stabilization (v1.0)
  2. React renderer (official support)
  3. Jetpack Compose and SwiftUI (native mobile)
  4. REST transport (production-ready)
  5. Integration with Genkit and LangGraph

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

A2UI represents a fundamental shift in how AI agents interact with users. Instead of text-only responses or risky code execution, agents can now describe rich, interactive UIs that render natively across platforms.

Key Takeaways:

  1. A2UI is declarative - It describes UIs, not executes code
  2. Security by design - Component catalog restricts what agents can request
  3. Framework-agnostic - One agent response works across web, mobile, desktop
  4. Composable - Works alongside MCP, AG-UI, and A2A for complete agent systems

As the agentic era accelerates, protocols like A2UI will become essential infrastructure for building AI applications that feel native, secure, and responsive.

Ready to build with A2UI? Start with the official quickstart or explore the GitHub samples.

Tags:
  • AI Agents
  • Google
  • A2UI
  • Agent Protocols
  • UI Generation
  • MCP
  • A2A

Hugging Face Skills: Fine-Tune Any Open-Source LLM with Natural Language

Dec 21, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Turn Conversations Into Fine-Tuned Models — No ML Expertise Required

What Are Hugging Face Skills?

Section titled “What Are Hugging Face Skills?”

Hugging Face Skills is a revolutionary system that enables AI coding agents like Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, and Google Gemini CLI to fine-tune open-source language models end-to-end through natural language conversations.

Instead of writing complex training scripts, configuring GPU resources, and managing deployment pipelines manually, you simply describe what you want in plain English:

The AI agent handles everything else: data validation, hardware selection, script generation, job submission, progress monitoring, and model publishing.

Key Features and Highlights

Section titled “Key Features and Highlights”

End-to-End Automation

Section titled “End-to-End Automation”
CapabilityDescription
Data ValidationAutomatically checks dataset format compatibility before training
Hardware SelectionIntelligently picks the right GPU based on model size and budget
Script GenerationCreates production-ready training scripts with TRL library
Job SubmissionSubmits training jobs to Hugging Face cloud GPUs
Real-time MonitoringTracks progress via Trackio dashboard
Model PublishingPushes finished models to Hugging Face Hub
GGUF ConversionConverts models for local deployment with llama.cpp/Ollama

Supported Training Methods

Section titled “Supported Training Methods”
MethodBest ForExample Dataset
SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning)Quality behavior examplesCustomer support conversations
DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)Preference-annotated dataChosen/rejected response pairs
GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization)Verifiable success criteriaMath problems, coding tasks

Multi-Platform Compatibility

Section titled “Multi-Platform Compatibility”
  • Anthropic Claude Code — Full integration via MCP protocol
  • OpenAI Codex — Uses AGENTS.md for instruction loading
  • Google Gemini CLI — Extensions-based integration
  • Coming Soon: Cursor, Windsurf, Continue

Prerequisites, Installation and Configuration

Section titled “Prerequisites, Installation and Configuration”

Requirements

Section titled “Requirements”
  • Hugging Face Account with Pro or Team/Enterprise plan (Jobs require paid plan)
  • Write-Access Token from huggingface.co/settings/tokens
  • Coding Agent (Claude Code, Codex, or Gemini CLI)

Claude Code Installation

Section titled “Claude Code Installation”

Alternative: MCP-based Installation

OpenAI Codex Installation

Section titled “OpenAI Codex Installation”

Codex auto-identifies skills via the AGENTS.md file:

Gemini CLI Installation

Section titled “Gemini CLI Installation”

Authentication

Section titled “Authentication”

Recommended Scenarios and Best Practices

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios and Best Practices”

Scenario 1: Customer Support Bot Fine-Tuning

Section titled “Scenario 1: Customer Support Bot Fine-Tuning”

Expected Workflow:

  1. Agent validates dataset format (looks for “messages” column)
  2. Selects t4-medium hardware (~$0.75/hour)
  3. Configures training with appropriate batch size
  4. Submits job and provides monitoring link
  5. Pushes model to your-username/qwen-support-sft

Scenario 2: Code Assistant with Preference Learning

Section titled “Scenario 2: Code Assistant with Preference Learning”

Multi-stage Pipeline:

  • First stage creates base capabilities
  • Second stage aligns with human preferences
  • Agent handles both stages automatically

Scenario 3: Math Reasoning with GRPO

Section titled “Scenario 3: Math Reasoning with GRPO”

Best for:

  • Problems with correct/incorrect answers
  • Code generation with unit tests
  • Mathematical proofs

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

  1. Always Validate First

    This costs pennies and prevents $30+ failures.

  2. Start with Demo Runs

  3. Be Explicit for Production

  4. Monitor in Real-Time

    Get live loss curves, learning rate, and ETA.

Hardware and Cost Guide

Section titled “Hardware and Cost Guide”
Model SizeRecommended GPUTraining TimeEst. CostNotes
<1Bt4-small<1 hour$1-2Demo/testing
1-3Bt4-medium / a10g-small2-3 hours$5-15Small production
3-7Ba10g-large (LoRA)4-8 hours$15-40Medium production
7B+Not supported——Too large for this tool

Note: Models above 3B automatically use LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) to fit in memory while maintaining quality.

Precautions, FAQ, and Troubleshooting

Section titled “Precautions, FAQ, and Troubleshooting”

Frequently Asked Questions

Section titled “Frequently Asked Questions”

Q: What’s the minimum cost to try this?

A: A demo run on 100 examples with t4-small costs approximately $0.30-0.50.

Q: Can I train models larger than 7B?

A: The current HF Skills system supports up to 7B. For larger models, use full Hugging Face infrastructure.

Q: Which dataset formats are supported?

A:

  • SFT: messages column with conversation format
  • DPO: chosen, rejected, and optionally prompt columns
  • GRPO: Dataset with verifiable correct answers

Q: How do I fix column name mismatches?

A: Ask the agent:

Common Issues and Solutions

Section titled “Common Issues and Solutions”
IssueSolution
Out of MemoryAgent suggests batch size reduction or hardware upgrade
Dataset Format ErrorRun validation first; agent identifies mismatches
Training TimeoutAdjust epoch count or use checkpointing
Authentication FailedVerify HF_TOKEN has write permissions

Pro Tips

Section titled “Pro Tips”
  1. Use Trackio Dashboard — Real-time training visualization at huggingface.co/spaces/username/trackio
  2. Convert to GGUF — Deploy locally without internet dependency
  3. Checkpoint Regularly — Recover from interruptions without losing progress

Comparison with Similar Products and Services

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Products and Services”

Hugging Face Skills vs. Traditional Fine-Tuning

Section titled “Hugging Face Skills vs. Traditional Fine-Tuning”
AspectTraditionalHF Skills
Setup TimeHours to daysMinutes
ML Knowledge RequiredExtensiveNone
Hardware ManagementManualAutomated
Cost VisibilityAfter trainingBefore submission
MonitoringCustom setupBuilt-in Trackio

Coding Agent Comparison

Section titled “Coding Agent Comparison”
AgentIntegration MethodStrengths
Claude CodeMCP + PluginBest for complex multi-step workflows
OpenAI CodexAGENTS.mdStrong code generation
Gemini CLIExtensionsGoogle ecosystem integration

Alternative Tools

Section titled “Alternative Tools”
ToolFocusKey Difference
Hugging Face AutoTrainGUI-based trainingVisual interface, less conversational
AWS SageMakerEnterprise MLMore complex, broader scope
Google Vertex AICloud MLTightly coupled with GCP
Together AIInference + TrainingFocus on API-first approach

Why Choose HF Skills?

  • Natural language interface
  • No code required
  • Cost-effective for small-to-medium models
  • Seamless Hub integration

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”

Official Resources

Section titled “Official Resources”
  • Hugging Face Skills GitHub Repository
  • Official Blog: We Got Claude to Fine-Tune an Open Source LLM
  • Hugging Face Jobs Documentation
  • TRL (Transformer Reinforcement Learning) Library
  • Trackio Monitoring

Coding Agent Documentation

Section titled “Coding Agent Documentation”
  • Claude Code Skills Guide
  • OpenAI Codex AGENTS.md Guide
  • Google Gemini CLI Extensions

Training Method References

Section titled “Training Method References”
  • SFT Training Methods
  • Hardware Selection Guide
  • GGUF Conversion Guide

Community Resources

Section titled “Community Resources”
  • Hugging Face MCP Course
  • Agents-MCP-Hackathon
  • Smithery MCP Server Registry

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Hugging Face Skills represents a paradigm shift in how we approach model fine-tuning. By bridging the gap between natural language and machine learning infrastructure, it democratizes access to custom AI model creation.

Whether you’re a developer looking to create a specialized code assistant, a business building a customer support bot, or a researcher exploring new training methods, HF Skills provides an accessible, cost-effective, and powerful solution.

Get Started Today:

The future of AI model training is conversational. Welcome to the age of Skills.

Last updated: December 21, 2025

Tags:
  • AI Tools
  • Hugging Face
  • LLM Training
  • Claude Code
  • Machine Learning
  • MCP

n8n-skills: Build Flawless n8n Workflows with Claude Code

Dec 21, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Transform Your n8n Workflow Development with AI-Powered Skills

What is n8n-skills?

Section titled “What is n8n-skills?”

n8n-skills is a collection of 7 complementary Claude Code skills designed to teach AI assistants how to build production-ready n8n workflows using the n8n-mcp MCP server.

Created by Romuald Członkowski from AI Advisors, these skills solve the common challenges developers face when building n8n workflows programmatically.

Why n8n-skills Exists

Section titled “Why n8n-skills Exists”

Building n8n workflows programmatically can be challenging. Common issues include:

ChallengeHow n8n-skills Helps
Using MCP tools incorrectlyExpert guidance on tool selection
Validation error loopsValidation error interpretation and fixing
Unknown workflow patterns5 proven architectural patterns
Node misconfigurationOperation-aware node configuration
Expression syntax errorsCorrect {{}} patterns and variables

The 7 Skills

Section titled “The 7 Skills”

1. n8n Expression Syntax

Section titled “1. n8n Expression Syntax”

Teaches correct n8n expression syntax and common patterns.

Activates when: Writing expressions, using {{}} syntax, accessing $json/$node variables.

Key Features:

  • Core variables ($json, $node, $now, $env)
  • Critical gotcha: Webhook data is under $json.body
  • Common mistakes catalog with fixes
  • When NOT to use expressions (Code nodes!)

2. n8n MCP Tools Expert (HIGHEST PRIORITY)

Section titled “2. n8n MCP Tools Expert (HIGHEST PRIORITY)”

Expert guide for using n8n-mcp MCP tools effectively.

Key Features:

  • Tool selection guide (which tool for which task)
  • nodeType format differences (nodes-base.* vs n8n-nodes-base.*)
  • Validation profiles (minimal/runtime/ai-friendly/strict)
  • Smart parameters (branch=“true” for IF nodes)
  • Auto-sanitization system explained

3. n8n Workflow Patterns

Section titled “3. n8n Workflow Patterns”

Build workflows using 5 proven architectural patterns.

Key Features:

  • 5 proven patterns (webhook processing, HTTP API, database, AI, scheduled)
  • Workflow creation checklist
  • Real examples from 2,653+ n8n templates
  • Pattern selection guide

4. n8n Validation Expert

Section titled “4. n8n Validation Expert”

Interpret validation errors and guide fixing.

Key Features:

  • Validation loop workflow
  • Real error catalog
  • Auto-sanitization behavior explained
  • False positives guide

5. n8n Node Configuration

Section titled “5. n8n Node Configuration”

Operation-aware node configuration guidance.

Key Features:

  • Property dependency rules (e.g., sendBody → contentType)
  • Operation-specific requirements
  • AI connection types (8 types for AI Agent workflows)

6. n8n Code JavaScript

Section titled “6. n8n Code JavaScript”

Write effective JavaScript code in n8n Code nodes.

Key Features:

  • Data access patterns ($input.all(), $input.first(), $input.item)
  • Correct return format: [{json: {...}}]
  • Built-in functions ($helpers.httpRequest(), DateTime, $jmespath())
  • Top 5 error patterns with solutions (covering 62%+ of failures)
  • 10 production-tested patterns

7. n8n Code Python

Section titled “7. n8n Code Python”

Write Python code in n8n Code nodes with proper limitations awareness.

Key Features:

  • Important: Use JavaScript for 95% of use cases
  • Critical limitation: No external libraries (requests, pandas, numpy)
  • Standard library reference (json, datetime, re, etc.)
  • Workarounds for missing libraries

Installation

Section titled “Installation”

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”
  1. n8n-mcp MCP server installed and configured (Installation Guide)
  2. Claude Code, Claude.ai, or Claude API access
  3. .mcp.json configured with n8n-mcp server

Method 1: Plugin Installation (Recommended)

Section titled “Method 1: Plugin Installation (Recommended)”

Method 2: Via Marketplace

Section titled “Method 2: Via Marketplace”

Method 3: Manual Installation

Section titled “Method 3: Manual Installation”

Recommended Scenarios

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios”

1. Building Webhook-to-Slack Workflows

Section titled “1. Building Webhook-to-Slack Workflows”

2. Creating AI Agent Workflows

Section titled “2. Creating AI Agent Workflows”

Perfect for building intelligent automation with LangChain nodes:

  • AI connection types guidance
  • Memory and output parser configuration
  • Streaming mode constraints

3. Scheduled Data Processing

Section titled “3. Scheduled Data Processing”

Build reliable scheduled workflows:

  • Scheduled trigger configuration
  • Database integration patterns
  • Error handling best practices

4. API Integration Workflows

Section titled “4. API Integration Workflows”

Connect external services efficiently:

  • HTTP Request node configuration
  • Authentication patterns
  • Response handling

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Start with Templates

Section titled “1. Start with Templates”

Always check n8n’s 2,709 workflow templates before building from scratch:

2. Validate Early and Often

Section titled “2. Validate Early and Often”

Use the multi-level validation approach:

LevelProfileUse Case
1minimalQuick required fields check (<100ms)
2runtimeFull validation with fixes
3ai-friendlyBalanced for AI workflows
4strictComplete validation

3. Understand Property Dependencies

Section titled “3. Understand Property Dependencies”

Many n8n nodes have conditional properties:

4. Use Correct Expression Syntax

Section titled “4. Use Correct Expression Syntax”

FAQs & Troubleshooting

Section titled “FAQs & Troubleshooting”

Q: Why does my webhook workflow fail?

Section titled “Q: Why does my webhook workflow fail?”

A: Remember that webhook data is under $json.body, not directly in $json. This is the most common gotcha.

Q: How do I choose between Code node JS vs Python?

Section titled “Q: How do I choose between Code node JS vs Python?”

A: Use JavaScript for 95% of use cases. Python has critical limitations:

  • No external libraries (requests, pandas, numpy)
  • Only standard library available

Q: Validation keeps failing with false positives

Section titled “Q: Validation keeps failing with false positives”

A: Use appropriate validation profiles:

  • minimal for quick checks during development
  • runtime for production-ready validation
  • Check the auto-sanitization behavior

Q: Which MCP tools should I use first?

Section titled “Q: Which MCP tools should I use first?”

A: Follow this order:

  1. search_templates - Check if templates exist
  2. search_nodes - Find required nodes
  3. get_node - Get configuration details
  4. validate_node - Validate your config
  5. validate_workflow - Final check

Product Comparison

Section titled “Product Comparison”

n8n-skills vs Building Manually

Section titled “n8n-skills vs Building Manually”
AspectWith n8n-skillsWithout
Learning curveGuided by AISelf-discovery
Error handlingProactive patternsTrial and error
Best practicesBuilt-inResearch required
ValidationMulti-level automatedManual testing

n8n vs Other Automation Tools

Section titled “n8n vs Other Automation Tools”
Featuren8nZapierMake
Self-hosted✅❌❌
Code nodes✅ Full JS/PythonLimitedLimited
AI integration✅ LangChainBasicBasic
Open source✅❌❌
MCP support✅ via n8n-mcp❌❌

Related Tools in the Ecosystem

Section titled “Related Tools in the Ecosystem”
ToolPurpose
n8n-mcpMCP server for n8n (required)
n8nWorkflow automation platform
Claude CodeAI coding assistant

What’s Included

Section titled “What’s Included”
  • 7 complementary skills that work together
  • 525+ n8n nodes supported
  • 2,653+ workflow templates for examples
  • 10 production-tested Code node patterns
  • Comprehensive error catalogs and troubleshooting guides

Summary

Section titled “Summary”

n8n-skills transforms the way you build n8n workflows with Claude Code:

  1. Automatic activation - Skills trigger when relevant queries are detected
  2. Composable design - All 7 skills work together seamlessly
  3. Production-ready patterns - Based on real-world usage
  4. Validation first - Catch errors before deployment

Whether you’re building simple webhook integrations or complex AI agent workflows, n8n-skills provides the guidance you need to build flawless automation.

Reference Links:

  • n8n-skills GitHub
  • n8n-mcp GitHub
  • n8n Official Documentation
  • n8n Workflow Templates
  • AI Advisors (Creator’s Website)

Ready to supercharge your n8n workflow development? Install n8n-skills today and let AI guide your automation journey!

  • AI Tools
  • n8n
  • Claude Code
  • Workflow Automation
  • MCP
  • Open Source

Claude Code + MCP: Auto-Publish Articles to WeChat Official Account in Minutes

Dec 20, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

What if you could go from a simple idea to a fully researched, professionally written article published to your WeChat Official Account - all in minutes, without leaving your terminal?

This isn’t science fiction. With Claude Code and the wenyan-mcp (Model Context Protocol) server, you can create an end-to-end content automation pipeline that:

  • Researches topics using web search
  • Generates high-quality bilingual articles
  • Automatically publishes to your WeChat draft box
  • Applies beautiful formatting themes

Let me show you exactly how to set this up.

What You’ll Need

Section titled “What You’ll Need”

Before we begin, make sure you have:

  1. Claude Code installed (v2.0+ recommended)
  2. Docker running on your machine
  3. WeChat Official Account with API credentials (AppID and AppSecret)

Step 1: Install Claude Code

Section titled “Step 1: Install Claude Code”

If you haven’t installed Claude Code yet:

Verify installation:

Step 2: Configure wenyan-mcp for WeChat

Section titled “Step 2: Configure wenyan-mcp for WeChat”

The magic happens through MCP (Model Context Protocol) - a standard that allows Claude to interact with external tools and services.

Add this configuration to your Claude Code MCP settings (~/.claude/settings.json or project-level .claude/settings.local.json):

Configuration Notes:

ParameterDescription
WECHAT_APP_IDYour WeChat Official Account AppID
WECHAT_APP_SECRETYour WeChat Official Account AppSecret
HOST_IMAGE_PATHLocal path for image storage (used for cover images)
-v mountMaps your local image directory to the container

Tip: Get your AppID and AppSecret from the WeChat Official Account Admin Platform.

Step 3: Create an Issue File

Section titled “Step 3: Create an Issue File”

The key to this workflow is defining your task in an issue file. This tells Claude exactly what you want to accomplish.

Create a file like engineering/00-issues/my-article-task.md:

Step 4: Execute the Workflow

Section titled “Step 4: Execute the Workflow”

Now comes the exciting part. Start Claude Code and reference your issue:

Then in the Claude Code prompt:

What Happens Next (Automatically)

Section titled “What Happens Next (Automatically)”

1. Research Phase

Section titled “1. Research Phase”

Claude searches the web for relevant, up-to-date information:

2. Content Synthesis

Section titled “2. Content Synthesis”

Claude fetches and analyzes multiple sources to create comprehensive content:

3. Article Generation & Publishing

Section titled “3. Article Generation & Publishing”

Claude generates the article and publishes directly to your WeChat draft box:

4. Review in WeChat

Section titled “4. Review in WeChat”

Open your WeChat Official Account admin panel - your article is waiting in the draft box:

Available Themes

Section titled “Available Themes”

The wenyan-mcp server supports multiple beautiful themes for your articles:

ThemeStyle
defaultClean, minimal
orangeheartWarm orange accents
rainbowColorful headings
lapisProfessional blue
piePlayful design
maizeGolden highlights
purpleElegant purple
phycatTech-focused

Specify your preferred theme when publishing:

Complete Workflow Summary

Section titled “Complete Workflow Summary”

Pro Tips

Section titled “Pro Tips”

1. Multi-Language Output

Section titled “1. Multi-Language Output”

Specify languages in your issue file:

Claude will generate both versions automatically.

2. Include Screenshots

Section titled “2. Include Screenshots”

Reference local images in your issue:

3. Customize Output Location

Section titled “3. Customize Output Location”

Specify where you want your blog files saved:

4. Review Before Final Publish

Section titled “4. Review Before Final Publish”

Articles go to your draft box first - you always have a chance to review and edit before final publication to subscribers.

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”
IssueSolution
Docker connection failedEnsure Docker Desktop is running
WeChat API errorVerify AppID/AppSecret are correct
Images not uploadingCheck HOST_IMAGE_PATH is correctly mapped
MCP server not foundRun claude mcp list to verify configuration

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

This workflow transforms content creation from a tedious multi-step process into a streamlined, automated pipeline. With Claude Code + wenyan-mcp, you can:

  • Save hours on research and writing
  • Maintain consistency across bilingual content
  • Publish directly without copy-paste gymnastics
  • Focus on ideas rather than execution

The future of content creation is here - and it lives in your terminal.

References

Section titled “References”
  • Claude Code Documentation
  • wenyan-mcp GitHub Repository
  • MCP Protocol Specification
  • WeChat Official Account API

  • AI Agent

Build a WeChat Chat Daily Report with Claude Code: chatlog + MCP in 3 Steps

Sep 18, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Turn yesterday’s group and private chats into a one‑click daily report: top keywords, key people, to‑dos, and risk alerts — fully automated.
This guide walks you through using Claude Code + chatlog to squeeze insights from chat data, with copy‑paste‑ready prompts.

What You’ll Get

Section titled “What You’ll Get”
  • End‑to‑end flow: export/decrypt → start HTTP → connect to Claude Code (MCP) → one‑click daily report
  • Prompt templates: ready to copy for data fetch, analysis/aggregation, and report generation (Markdown/HTML/images)
  • Best practices: keyword radar, sensitive‑term alerts, to‑do extraction, sentiment curve
  • Pitfall checklist: keys, SIP, remote/local connectors, CSV/audio

chatlog is an open‑source tool that supports local WeChat database access, automatic decryption, HTTP API, Webhook, and MCP Streamable HTTP, so Claude Code can use it directly as a “data tool”.

1. Setup (10 minutes)

Section titled “1. Setup (10 minutes)”

1) Install and start chatlog (local)

Section titled “1) Install and start chatlog (local)”

In the TUI, select Decrypt Data → Start HTTP Service (default http://127.0.0.1:5030).
API examples:

Supports YYYY-MM-DD or YYYY-MM-DD~YYYY-MM-DD windows; format supports json/csv/text.

Tip: First obtain the data key/image key. On macOS, you may need to temporarily disable SIP to extract keys (re‑enable afterwards).

2) Expose chatlog to Claude Code as an MCP server

Section titled “2) Expose chatlog to Claude Code as an MCP server”

chatlog natively supports MCP Streamable HTTP. Once HTTP is running, the endpoint is:

Add a remote MCP in Claude Code:

Then in Claude Code, use /mcp to manage and use tools.

MCP is an open standard by Anthropic with Streamable HTTP transport and OAuth/Token auth, making it easy to plug external data into AI assistants.

Note: Remote MCP support is being rolled out. You can also use local STDIO. Capabilities depend on your account and version.

2. A low‑friction daily report structure (Claude can generate it directly)

Section titled “2. A low‑friction daily report structure (Claude can generate it directly)”
  • Today’s overview: total messages, active sessions, top groups/DMs, peak hours
  • Keyword radar: TF‑IDF/key phrases/topic clustering (merge synonyms)
  • Task extraction: a to‑do list triggered by phrases like “tomorrow/tonight/please/help me/to‑do/schedule”
  • Risk alerts: custom sensitive/compliance term hits with counts and context
  • Sentiment curve: hourly distribution (positive/neutral/negative) with anomalous peaks highlighted
  • High‑value snippets: top 5 highest information‑density excerpts (with session name/timestamp/message ID)
  • Appendix: raw data links (APIs can deep‑link to decrypted images/files/audio streams)

3. Claude Code prompts (copy‑paste ready)

Section titled “3. Claude Code prompts (copy‑paste ready)”

Usage: replace variables in {}; paste directly into Claude Code.
Below includes the recommended MCP approach and an HTTP‑only fallback (curl/script).

A. Connection check & list tools

Section titled “A. Connection check & list tools”

B. Fetch all messages from yesterday (grouped by session)

Section titled “B. Fetch all messages from yesterday (grouped by session)”

(API shape per README; chatlog dynamically decrypts/serves media as accessible paths.)

C. Keyword radar & to‑do extraction

Section titled “C. Keyword radar & to‑do extraction”

D. Sentiment and peak hours

Section titled “D. Sentiment and peak hours”

E. Generate the report (Markdown + HTML + images)

Section titled “E. Generate the report (Markdown + HTML + images)”

F. Quick HTTP‑only fallback (no MCP required)

Section titled “F. Quick HTTP‑only fallback (no MCP required)”

API params and media decryption/serving are provided by chatlog.

4. Best practices: make the report smarter

Section titled “4. Best practices: make the report smarter”
  1. Synonym map: normalize common colloquialisms to reduce word‑cloud noise.
  2. To‑do extraction rules: regex + few‑shot tuned to phrases like “help me”, “remember to”, “by tonight”.
  3. Role profiling: score members by talk density/influence to spot “information sources”.
  4. Webhook increments: pair chatlog’s Webhook with your middleware to push deltas as new messages arrive.
  5. CSV/audio: recent versions improved CSV and audio pipelines — check the Releases Changelog.

5. Pitfall checklist

Section titled “5. Pitfall checklist”
  • Only process data you legally own. Respect privacy and platform terms; the project emphasizes this as well.
  • On macOS, obtaining keys may require temporarily disabling SIP; re‑enable afterwards.
  • Remote MCP support is rolling out; local STDIO is more stable. Check your account/version.
  • MCP connection syntax follows the official docs: claude mcp add --transport http <name> <url>.
  • MCP concepts/auth: use Streamable HTTP with standard auth for safer team deployments.

6. Advanced: portable sources & team sharing

Section titled “6. Advanced: portable sources & team sharing”
  • Deploy chatlog with Docker on a NAS and pre‑set CHATLOG_WEBHOOK_* env vars to integrate your toolchain.
  • Use Claude Connectors/MCP catalogs to manage multiple sources so non‑technical teammates can connect with a click.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Treat chats as data and use Claude Code to refine them into an actionable team daily.
Spend 3 minutes skimming each day and get time back for what actually matters.

Tags:
  • Claude Code
  • chatlog
  • MCP
  • WeChat

Plug MCP into Codex: 10 Must‑Have Servers with Copy‑Paste Configs

Sep 10, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Add MCP servers to ~/.codex/config.toml, and Codex suddenly “grows hands”: it can search the web, read docs, click around in a browser, and edit code. This guide gives you copy‑paste configs, practical use cases, and gotchas.

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

We often ask models to write code, research, and run tasks—but they used to lack “real‑world” tooling. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is like USB‑C for AI: a unified way to attach external tools so the agent can use them on demand. OpenAI describes MCP as “a USB‑C port for AI”—plug in and go.

Microsoft’s Playwright MCP is on board too: an agent can actually launch a browser, inspect the accessibility tree, and verify changes—no reliance on screenshots.

1‑Minute Setup: ~/.codex/config.toml

Section titled “1‑Minute Setup: ~/.codex/config.toml”

In Codex CLI, your MCP servers live under the mcp_servers section (TOML). Minimal shape:

Recent Codex releases support per‑server startup timeouts (startup_timeout_ms). Release notes and community reports mention a default around 10s; raising it improves stability on cold starts or slower networks.

Tip: Many MCP clients prefer STDIO transport. If you hit a server that only supports SSE, bridge it with mcp‑proxy (STDIO ↔ SSE in both directions).

Copy‑Paste List: 10 High‑Leverage MCP Servers

Section titled “Copy‑Paste List: 10 High‑Leverage MCP Servers”

Each block includes purpose, a ready‑to‑paste config, and key notes. Drop blocks into ~/.codex/config.toml. Replace env vars with your own keys.

1) Context7 — Stream current docs and samples into context

Section titled “1) Context7 — Stream current docs and samples into context”

Purpose: Pull up‑to‑date official docs and examples (by version) to reduce stale APIs and hallucinations.

Notes: Works with multiple IDE clients; their docs include samples for Cursor/VS Code/Zed/Claude.

2) DeepWiki — Q&A and structured knowledge for GitHub repos

Section titled “2) DeepWiki — Q&A and structured knowledge for GitHub repos”

Purpose: Query DeepWiki‑indexed open‑source repos; read wiki content and structure directly.

Notes: Free, no login required; you can submit your repo for indexing.

3) Playwright — Actually open a browser for E2E checks

Section titled “3) Playwright — Actually open a browser for E2E checks”

Purpose: Page interactions, accessibility tree, scripts, and automated tests.

Notes: Actively maintained; great for regression validation, visual checks, and end‑to‑end tests.

4) Exa — Real‑time web search with structured results

Section titled “4) Exa — Real‑time web search with structured results”

Purpose: Use the Exa Search API for live retrieval, returning structured titles/URLs/summaries—ideal for research.

Notes: Docs and NPM package are actively updated; hosted versions exist.

5) Spec‑Workflow — A full spec‑driven development pipeline

Section titled “5) Spec‑Workflow — A full spec‑driven development pipeline”

Purpose: Drive projects with Requirements → Design → Tasks → Implementation, complete with dashboards and a VS Code extension.

Notes: Official examples include one‑tap config; great for multi‑person collaboration with approval trails.

6) Sequential‑Thinking — Structured reasoning with branches

Section titled “6) Sequential‑Thinking — Structured reasoning with branches”

Purpose: Break complex problems into steps, enable branching and dynamic “thought” counts with traceability.

Notes: Multiple server implementations; ideal where explainable, stepwise plans matter.

7) Magic (21st.dev) — Generate production‑grade UI components from natural language

Section titled “7) Magic (21st.dev) — Generate production‑grade UI components from natural language”

Purpose: Produce production‑ready React components from prompts; supports Cursor/Windsurf/VS Code.

Notes: Clear setup and usage; great for tight design‑to‑dev loops.

8) Serena — Semantic code search + symbol‑level edits (IDE‑like power)

Section titled “8) Serena — Semantic code search + symbol‑level edits (IDE‑like power)”

Purpose: LSP‑backed semantic understanding and symbol‑level modifications across languages—perfect for large‑repo refactors and precise edits.

Notes: Open source and active; release notes emphasize stability and sync execution.

9) Morph Fast‑Apply — Ultra‑fast, reliable patch application (4k+ tokens/s)

Section titled “9) Morph Fast‑Apply — Ultra‑fast, reliable patch application (4k+ tokens/s)”

Purpose: Apply model‑proposed diffs to real files quickly and safely; great for large‑scale refactors and batch fixes.

Notes: Supports a restricted “edit‑only” mode or full filesystem tools.

10) Open‑Web‑Search — Multi‑engine web search (STDIO/SSE)

Section titled “10) Open‑Web‑Search — Multi‑engine web search (STDIO/SSE)”

Purpose: Unified access to DuckDuckGo/Bing/Brave; switch engines and transport modes with one config.

Notes: The repo documents MODE=stdio|sse and default search engine settings.

How do I connect remote/SSE servers? Use mcp‑proxy.

Section titled “How do I connect remote/SSE servers? Use mcp‑proxy.”

Many clients prefer STDIO for local executables. For remote SSE servers, bridge with mcp‑proxy:

  • Mode A: STDIO → SSE (let a STDIO‑only client reach a remote SSE server)
  • Mode B: SSE → STDIO (expose a local STDIO server as a remote SSE endpoint)

One‑liner example:
mcp-proxy --port=8080 -- npx @playwright/mcp@latest (expose local STDIO Playwright as SSE)

Security Tips (Read this!)

Section titled “Security Tips (Read this!)”
  • Least privilege: only pass the env vars and directory access each server truly needs.
  • Supply chain hygiene: the ecosystem is growing fast, and research highlights “parasitic toolchain attacks” as an emerging risk. Prefer official or trusted sources; isolate sensitive data.

Common Issues & Troubleshooting

Section titled “Common Issues & Troubleshooting”
  • Tool startup timeouts: raise startup_timeout_ms (e.g., 20000). This is supported per server and helps with slow networks or cold starts.
  • SSE won’t connect: check whether you need mcp‑proxy or a gateway in between.
  • Playwright install quirks: if a package version fails, try @latest or track open issues.

Closing

Section titled “Closing”

Plugging MCP into Codex is like staffing a mini team: someone fetches docs, someone drives the browser, someone edits code, someone orchestrates the workflow. The 10 blocks above can be pasted and run as‑is, and with mcp‑proxy, both local and remote setups are straightforward.

Which ones will you connect first, and what problems will they solve for you? Share your combo in the comments.

Tags:
  • Codex
  • MCP
  • OpenAI
  • Context7
  • Playwright
  • DeepWiki
  • Exa
  • Spec-Workflow
  • Sequential-Thinking
  • Magic
  • Serena
  • Morph
  • Open-Web-Search
  • mcp-proxy

terminalcp lets Claude Code manage Terminal sessions like Playwright

Aug 13, 2025
Vibe Sparking

In one sentence: terminalcp = Playwright for the terminal.
It lets an AI agent start, operate, and read any command‑line tool (LLDB/GDB, Python REPL, database shells, even other AI CLIs) like a human—and lets you and me take over the same session at any time.

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

You may already use Playwright/Browser for web automation—so what about an “automation console” for the command line?
terminalcp abstracts “what a human does in the terminal” into an MCP tool: start a process → send keystrokes → read output → manage multiple sessions, and also supports socket attach so you can watch and take over the AI’s actions in real time.

Why add it to your toolchain?

Section titled “Why add it to your toolchain?”

1) Truly interactive control
Not just “run a command and exit,” but persistent sessions: single‑step in LLDB/GDB, try code in a REPL, or view the “full screen” in TUI apps like vim/htop.

2) Two output modes, pick as needed

  • Terminal mode (stdout): captures the rendered screen buffer, perfect for debuggers and TUIs.
  • Stream mode: captures the raw text stream (ANSI stripped by default), and supports incremental reads with since_last—more efficient for builds/logs.

3) High‑fidelity PTY
Processes run in a pseudo‑terminal, preserving colors/cursor moves/special key sequences—just like human input; plus it manages multiple processes in the background so your agent doesn’t block.

4) Socket attach: the key to human–AI collaboration
Each process automatically exposes a Unix domain socket. You can attach to an already‑running AI session to co‑view and co‑operate, jumping in to help anytime.

5) More “AI‑friendly” than screen/tmux

  • Structured responses (JSON) instead of a wall of text.
  • Clean output (ANSI can be stripped), easier to feed into an LLM.
  • Incremental reads and lifecycle management (exit codes, etc.) built in.

Install and quick start (works with any MCP client)

Section titled “Install and quick start (works with any MCP client)”

Standard config (works for most tools)
Add the server in your MCP client:

Claude Code (CLI)

Cursor
Settings → MCP → Add new MCP Server
Name: terminalcp, Type: command, Command:
npx @mariozechner/terminalcp@latest.

VS Code (incl. Copilot Agent)

After installation, GitHub Copilot Agent in VS Code can use it directly.

Windsurf / other MCP clients
Follow their MCP docs with the “standard config” above, or install globally first:

Then configure:

Requirements: Node.js ≥ 18; any MCP‑capable client (VS Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Desktop, Goose, etc.).

How does it work with Claude Code / Cursor?

Section titled “How does it work with Claude Code / Cursor?”

Principle: terminalcp exposes a single MCP tool terminal, and you control sessions via JSON commands: start / stdin / stdout / stream / stop / list / term-size.

Workflow A: Let Claude control another AI CLI

Section titled “Workflow A: Let Claude control another AI CLI”
  1. Start Claude/Gemini CLIs (use an absolute path; shell aliases won’t work):
  1. Send input (auto submit/Enter):
  1. Read the full screen (great for TUI/debugger UIs):
  1. Clean up the process:

Shell aliases don’t apply; for interactive CLIs prefer submit:true; remember to stop when done.

Workflow B: Tail builds/logs incrementally

Section titled “Workflow B: Tail builds/logs incrementally”

ANSI is stripped by default; set strip_ansi:false if you need color codes.

Workflow C: REPL / debugging

Section titled “Workflow C: REPL / debugging”

Python REPL

LLDB

Collaboration superpower: socket attach (tmux‑like, but smarter)

Section titled “Collaboration superpower: socket attach (tmux‑like, but smarter)”

Name the process when starting it:

From your local terminal, list and attach:

  • Operate together in your own terminal;
  • Ctrl+Q to detach without killing the session;
  • Supports multiple concurrent attachments;
  • Socket paths live under ~/.terminalcp/sessions/.

How it differs from screen/tmux (tested: better for AI)

Section titled “How it differs from screen/tmux (tested: better for AI)”
  • Structured responses: JSON replies that are easy for agents to parse.
  • Reliable output capture: stdout gives the rendered screen; stream supports incremental “since_last” reads.
  • Less noise: ANSI control codes stripped by default so escape sequences don’t swamp your context.
  • State‑aware: detects process exit and error codes.
  • No guessing timing: avoids brittle sleep‑style “maybe it’s done” waits.
    You can “simulate” some of this with tmux/screen, but it’s tedious and fragile.

Best‑practice checklist

Section titled “Best‑practice checklist”
  • Use absolute paths for CLIs (e.g., Claude CLI); shell aliases don’t apply.
  • For interactive commands, use {"submit":true}—or send text and \r as two steps.
  • Use stream + since_last for long tasks to avoid re‑pulling full logs.
  • Name sessions + attach via sockets for quick collaboration and fire‑fighting.
  • Explicitly stop when finished; on MCP client exit, leftover processes are auto‑cleaned.
  • Licensed MIT—safe to integrate into internal toolchains.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

If you’re building AI‑assisted development / internal DevTools / AIOps, terminalcp turns “everything in the terminal” into an orchestrable API: agent control, human–agent co‑operation, automation with visibility. Starting today, let your AI not just “run commands,” but actually “use the terminal.”

Where will you plug it in first?
Build logs, kernel debugging, on‑call incident response—or turning Claude/Gemini into controllable “sub‑agents”? What do you think?

📦 Appendix: copy‑and‑paste quick reference

Section titled “📦 Appendix: copy‑and‑paste quick reference”

MCP standard config (works for most clients)

Claude Code CLI

VS Code CLI

Global install (optional)

Tags:
  • terminalcp
  • AI
  • MCP
  • Terminal
  • Tmux
  • Screen
